title: Factor repeated or substantial arguments into helper lemmas
body: Extract repeated reasoning or nontrivial subproofs into helper lemmas, often `private` if they are not public API. But avoid ad hoc single-use lemmas unless they materially improve readability or reuse.

title: Reuse existing lemmas and APIs before writing long proofs
body:
If a proof is longer than expected, first search for a theorem that already expresses the needed fact, perhaps after a small rewrite. Prefer `exact`, `apply`, `rw`, `simpa`, `.mp`, or `.mpr` over reproving standard facts.
```lean
by
  simpa using existing_lemma h
```

title: Prefer targeted rewriting and canonical normal forms
body: Use focused `rw`, `simp only [...]`, or local simplification at the relevant hypothesis or goal rather than broad simplification. Rewrite into standard forms such as `mem_*_iff` or other canonical API shapes early.

title: Prefer explicit API lemmas over fragile definitional equality
body:
Do not rely on accidental definitional equality across wrappers, coercions, or implementation details in library-facing proofs. Prove and use named lemmas about the abstraction instead.
```lean
rw [foo_def]
exact h
```

title: Prefer clear proof structure over golfing
body: Write proofs so the main argument is easy to follow. Avoid dense one-liners, deep nesting, giant `exact` terms, or brittle automation when a clearer structured proof is available.

title: Use `rfl` only for genuine definitional equalities
body: If the goal is truly definitional, close it with `rfl` or `Iff.rfl`, preferably directly. Do not force “heavy `rfl`” on large terms when a small rewrite is clearer or more robust.

title: Prefer high-level domain-specific APIs over element-chasing
body: For algebraic, categorical, topological, set-theoretic, filter, and subobject arguments, use the standard library combinators, homomorphism lemmas, universal properties, basis lemmas, and extensionality principles rather than manual element-chasing.

title: Keep theorem hypotheses minimal
body: Remove unused assumptions. Do not require stronger hypotheses, extra typeclasses, or side conditions unless the proof genuinely needs them.

title: Prefer rewriting lemmas over `unfold`
body: Avoid broad `unfold` in proofs. Add and use named lemmas such as `foo_def` or `foo_apply` so rewriting is explicit and stable.

title: Use extensionality lemmas for equality of functions and structures
body:
For equality of functions, morphisms, sets, or structures, use `ext`, `funext`, `SetLike.ext`, `LinearMap.ext`, and similar lemmas rather than unfolding definitions or doing indirect equality proofs.
```lean
ext x
simp
```

title: Prefer `simpa`/`simpa using` for short adaptations
body: If a proof is just an existing lemma plus harmless rewriting, use `simpa` rather than a longer `rw`/`apply`/`exact` sequence.

title: Unfold only what you need, and prefer `dsimp` for definitional simplification
body: When unfolding is necessary, target the exact constant with `dsimp only [...]` or a narrow `unfold`. Use `dsimp` for pure definitional simplification and `simp` for lemma-based simplification.

title: Add explicit types or arguments when elaboration is fragile
body:
If coercions, dependent types, or inference make a proof hard to read or unstable, add type annotations, named arguments, or explicit application grouping.
```lean
rw [lemma_name (α := α) (f := f)]
```

title: Inline tiny one-use helpers
body: If a helper lemma or `have` is just a short instantiation of existing lemmas and used once, inline it instead of creating extra declarations.

title: State lemmas in a canonical, reusable, and appropriately general form
body: Keep theorem statements short and in the normal API form. Avoid awkward coercions, wrappers, contrapositive formulations, or unnecessary specialization; prove the most natural useful statement and derive variants as corollaries.

title: Use `suffices` to expose the key intermediate goal
body:
When the proof naturally proceeds through a simpler or more canonical intermediate claim, state it with `suffices`. This is often clearer than a long chain of local rewrites.
```lean
by
  suffices h : p = ⊥ by simpa [h]
  ...
```

title: Use standard case-split idioms
body: For simple decidable splits, use idioms like `by_cases h : x = 0` or `obtain rfl | h := eq_or_ne x 0`. For order splits, use standard lemmas such as `le_total`, `lt_or_ge`, or `le_or_gt`.

title: Use `have` for facts, `let` for data, and `letI`/`haveI` for local instances
body: Record reusable propositions with `have`, name terms with `let`, and introduce proof-local instances with `letI` or `haveI`. Do not pollute theorem statements with local implementation details.

title: Shape goals explicitly with `change`, `show`, `convert`, or `convert_to` when needed
body:
If a lemma almost matches but the goal is in the wrong syntactic form, reshape the goal explicitly rather than forcing awkward rewrites. Use these tools deliberately, not as a substitute for missing API.
```lean
change ((x : ℤ) ≤ y)
convert h using 1
```

title: Prefer the simplest applicable specialized lemma or tactic
body: Use the straightforward API lemma or domain tactic first, such as `positivity`, `norm_num`, `ring`, `linarith`, `omega`, `norm_cast`, or `field_simp`. Reach for more technical variants only if needed.

title: Add focused regression tests for subtle tactic or elaboration behavior
body: When fixing or extending tactics, macros, simprocs, or elaboration behavior, add small explicit tests that capture the bug and nearby edge cases.

title: Use `calc` for chained equalities and inequalities
body:
When a proof is naturally a sequence of algebraic or order-theoretic steps, use a `calc` block rather than repeated `rw`, `trans`, or transitivity lemmas.
```lean
calc
  x ≤ y := hxy
  _ = z := hz
  _ < t := hzt
```

title: Structure `↔` proofs explicitly
body: For iff goals, keep the two directions visibly separate using `constructor`, `refine ⟨..., ...⟩`, or `where mp := ...; mpr := ...`.

title: Prefer universal properties and natural isomorphisms over forced equalities
body: For categorical or abstract constructions, use the universal property and the corresponding API. State isomorphisms or equivalences rather than equalities when objects are naturally isomorphic, and prove equalities componentwise with `ext` when appropriate.

title: Prefer standard induction principles, with explicit generalization when needed
body:
Use the type’s intended induction principle, and be explicit about `generalizing`, `revert`, or `generalize` so the induction hypothesis has the right strength.
```lean
induction n generalizing k with
| zero => ...
| succ n ih => ...
```

title: Prefer `obtain`/`rcases` for witnesses and `let` for data
body: Use `let` to name computed terms, and use `obtain`/`rcases`/`cases` to extract witnesses or components from hypotheses. Avoid `choose` unless it is genuinely the right tool.

title: Avoid unnecessary case splits and duplicated branch work
body: Do not introduce `by_cases`, pattern matches, or large case analyses unless they contribute to the argument. If branches share setup, move that work outside the split or package it in a common `have`.

title: Handle trivial or degenerate cases early
body: Split off easy cases like `x = 0`, `n = 0`, `s = ∅`, `⊥`, or `⊤` at the start so the main proof stays clean. Close such branches immediately with `simp`, `rfl`, or a standard lemma when possible.

title: Use standard proof patterns for set and subobject equality
body: For set and subobject equalities, prefer canonical patterns such as `ext x; simp` or `SetLike.ext`. Use propositional automation only as final cleanup, not as a substitute for structure.

title: Destructure hypotheses and witnesses early
body:
Use `obtain`, `rcases`, `rintro`, or pattern matching to extract the components you need from `∃`, `And`, `Or`, and similar hypotheses. Do not repeatedly write `.1`, `.2`, or projection tricks.
```lean
obtain ⟨x, hx, hy⟩ := h
```

title: Orient rewrite lemmas and simp lemmas toward simplification
body: State equalities with the more complicated expression on the left and the simpler normal form on the right. Do the same for `iff` lemmas intended for rewriting.

title: Prefer explicit constructors and witnesses
body:
When proving existential, product, subtype, or `iff` goals, use `refine ⟨...⟩`, `use`, `constructor`, or direct constructor syntax so the subgoals are visible.
```lean
by
  refine ⟨?_, ?_⟩
```

title: Add basic API lemmas after new definitions
body: After introducing a definition, provide the obvious `rfl`/`_def`/`_apply` lemmas and basic `@[simp]` lemmas for constructors, projections, coercions, and applications.

title: Keep the main proof branch visually prominent
body: Follow the line-of-sight rule: subordinate branches may be indented, but the main proof flow should remain easy to scan. Use bullets or `case` blocks to separate subgoals clearly.

title: Separate rewriting from discharge tactics
body: When using tactics like `field_simp`, `simp`, or algebraic normalization, structure the proof in phases: first rewrite or normalize, then solve the resulting goals. Keep side conditions visible and explicit.

title: Keep proof style consistent within a proof
body: Do not mix tactic mode and term mode in a small expression unless it clearly improves readability. Avoid deeply nested `by` blocks when a direct style works.

title: Use filter and eventual-equality APIs idiomatically
body: For filter, almost-everywhere, or eventual arguments, use `filter_upwards`, `EventuallyEq`, and standard congruence lemmas rather than manually unpacking filter definitions.

title: Prefer `.mp` / `.mpr` when using one direction of an `iff`
body: If you need only one direction of an iff lemma, apply `.mp` or `.mpr` directly instead of rewriting the whole goal.

title: Keep one active goal at a time and discharge subgoals explicitly
body: Avoid leaving many goals open simultaneously. Use bullets, `case`, `all_goals`, helper lemmas, or explicit structure so every generated subgoal is handled clearly.

title: Prefer `exact` when you already have the term, and `refine` when you want visible subgoals
body:
Use `exact` when the required term is already known. Prefer `refine` over `apply` when it makes the remaining obligations clearer.
```lean
refine ⟨?_, ?_⟩
```

title: Control expensive unfolding and computation when performance matters
body: If elaboration or kernel checking is slow because a definition unfolds aggressively, factor expensive computation into separate definitions or use appropriate opacity wrappers. Prefer source-level fixes over brittle local hacks.

title: Keep classical reasoning local
body: Do not add `Classical` or decidability assumptions to theorem statements unless required by the statement itself. Use `by classical` inside the proof instead.

title: Design simp lemmas carefully and keep simp local when needed
body: Add explicit `@[simp]` lemmas for intended rewriting behavior, but avoid simp loops and unstable rewrite directions. For delicate projection or coercion goals, prefer local simplification over broad global simp behavior.

title: Fix the root cause of automation failures
body: If `simp`, `to_additive`, `reassoc`, `norm_cast`, or similar tools fail downstream, fix the underlying attribute, lemma, or generated theorem when possible rather than patching many individual proofs.

title: Keep rewrite chains readable
body: If many rewrites are needed, use a `calc` block or intermediate `have`s rather than a long opaque `rw [...]` chain. Avoid tricks like `rw [show ... by ...]` when a clearer structure is available.

title: State and discharge side conditions explicitly for partial operations
body: For division, inverses, roots, and similar operations, add the needed nonzero or nonnegativity assumptions explicitly. Do not rely on hidden side-condition discharge.

title: Introduce local instances explicitly with `letI`/`haveI`
body: If a proof needs a typeclass instance that is not inferable from the context, add it locally with `letI` or `haveI`. Do not rely on accidental inference through unrelated variables.

title: Keep ports incremental and avoid unrelated cleanup
body: When porting Lean 3 or older mathlib code, first get the file compiling with minimal semantic changes. Defer stylistic refactors and unrelated cleanup unless they are required to make progress.

title: Use coercion and cast lemmas deliberately
body: For cast-heavy goals, first align the syntax with `change` if needed, then use `norm_cast`, `push_cast`, or explicit coercion lemmas. Do not rely on implicit coercions or magical cast normalization.

title: Add `@[ext]` lemmas for new structures when appropriate
body: If a structure has a natural extensionality principle, provide an `@[ext]` lemma in the normal form users naturally need.

title: Handle dependent rewriting explicitly
body: When later arguments depend on earlier equalities, prefer `subst`, `rintro rfl`, `obtain rfl`, `dsimp`, or explicit cast lemmas. Do not hope that `simp` will magically solve dependent rewrites.

title: Use antisymmetry lemmas for equality of ordered objects
body: For set, submodule, subgroup, or lattice equalities, prove both inclusions or inequalities and conclude with `subset_antisymm`, `le_antisymm`, or similar lemmas.

title: Prefer direct contradiction tools over awkward case splits
body: Use `contrapose`, `contrapose!`, `mt`, `Or.resolve_left`, and similar tools when they match the argument better than a `by_cases` that immediately leads to contradiction.

title: Use local abbreviations sparingly and purposefully
body: Introduce `let` or `set` only when it clarifies a recurring expression, controls normalization, or improves proof structure. Avoid throwaway local definitions that obscure the main argument.

title: Design definitions to compute well
body: Choose definitions so key projections or evaluations are `rfl` or reduce by simple computation when possible. Separate computational data from later proof obligations so downstream proofs do not depend on opaque machinery.

title: Keep abstraction boundaries opaque
body: Do not unfold wrappers, type synonyms, structures, or proof terms just to make a proof go through unless there is a compelling reason. Prove and use lemmas about the abstraction instead of exposing implementation details.

title: Prefer quotient APIs and direct elimination of impossible cases
body: For quotient-like types, use the provided induction, recursion, and universal-property APIs rather than extracting representatives ad hoc. For vacuous branches, use idiomatic eliminators like `False.elim`, `Fin.elim0`, or `rintro ⟨⟩`.

title: Build structures and instances directly and reuse inherited fields
body: When defining bundled structures or instances, construct them explicitly with the target constructor and reuse inherited operations and proofs whenever possible. Avoid reproving obvious superclass fields or relying on fragile derived machinery.

title: Structure proofs so each step addresses the current goal
body: Write proofs so each tactic block visibly solves the goal in front of it. Avoid scripts that depend on fragile goal ordering or on later tactics accidentally solving earlier goals.

title: Put binders and hypotheses in the statement when natural
body:
If the proof immediately introduces variables or assumptions, move them before the colon rather than starting with `intro` boilerplate.
```lean
theorem foo (h : P) : Q := ...
```

title: Use descriptive names for local hypotheses and facts
body: Name hypotheses and intermediate facts clearly, such as `hx`, `hmem`, or `hmono`. Avoid overuse of `this`, anonymous assumptions, or repeated projection chains like `h.1.2`.

title: Keep automation targeted and readable
body: Use automation only after the goal has been put into a clean, stable shape. Do not use `aesop`, `simp`, or similar tactics as glorified `intro`/`constructor`, and prefer small `simp only` sets or targeted rewrites for intermediate steps.

title: Remove unused hypotheses and unnecessary intermediate facts
body: Treat linter warnings such as `unusedHavesSuffices` as prompts to simplify the proof. Delete irrelevant assumptions and avoid long walls of `have` statements that obscure the real dependencies.

title: Prefer direct construction for definitions and objects
body: Do not define objects by tactic scripts that merely prove existence. Construct them directly so definitional behavior is good and later proofs are simpler.

title: Use `subst` early for equalities of variables
body: If a hypothesis identifies a local variable, eliminate it early with `subst`, `rintro rfl`, or `obtain rfl`. This often simplifies the context and removes awkward casts.

title: Prefer structural recursion and honest termination arguments
body: Define recursive functions by structural recursion whenever possible. If well-founded recursion is necessary, use `termination_by` with a measure that matches the actual recursive decrease, and avoid `partial` for definitions you will reason about.

title: Use `Subsingleton.elim` in subsingleton contexts
body: When proving equality in a subsingleton type, use `Subsingleton.elim` rather than forcing progress with congruence or extensionality.

title: Avoid blank lines inside proofs
body: Do not use empty lines inside proof blocks. If you need visual separation, use proof structure or a brief comment instead.

title: Induct on the actual structure, not a proxy
body: Induct on the list, finset, quotient witness, or natural number itself rather than on length or another derived measure unless that measure is essential.

title: Keep induction cases short and clear
body: In inductive proofs, handle base and step cases with focused `simp` and the induction hypothesis when possible. Use explicit case names rather than relying on fragile goal renaming.

title: Use categorical reassociation, naturality, and cancellation lemmas
body: In category-theoretic proofs, prefer `[reassoc]` lemmas, naturality lemmas, `cancel_mono`, and `cancel_epi` over long manual associativity rewrites.

title: Avoid unnecessary coercions in theorem statements
body: Keep theorem statements at the natural abstraction level. Do not coerce morphisms, subobjects, or bundled structures to concrete types unless necessary.

title: Prefer `Finset`/`Finite` APIs when finiteness is central
body: If the argument is fundamentally finite, use `Finset`, `Finite`, and their APIs rather than set-theoretic encodings that obscure finiteness.

title: Add comments sparingly but usefully
body: Do not clutter short proofs with comments, but add brief comments for long proofs or non-obvious strategy shifts so the overall argument is clear.

title: Refactor for structure before micro-optimizing performance
body: If a proof or file is slow, first reduce duplication, simplify elaboration, and improve structure. Diagnose the source of slow `simp`, typeclass search, or unfolding before adding ad hoc workarounds.

title: Prefer `↔` when the result is genuinely bidirectional
body: If both directions are natural and useful, state an `iff`. If only one direction matters, do not force an `iff`.

title: Order binders and hypotheses for readability
body: Put explicit data before dependent hypotheses, and structural assumptions before side conditions. Keep argument order aligned with common usage.

title: Prefer separate assumptions over conjunction-packed hypotheses
body: Use separate named assumptions instead of a single `h : A ∧ B ∧ C` unless the bundled form is mathematically canonical.

title: Do not add tautological or purely definitional lemmas without API value
body: Avoid lemmas whose statement is just a syntactic tautology or trivial `rfl` unless the named lemma materially improves rewriting, discoverability, or the public API.

title: Prefer `Finite` over `Fintype` in statements when possible
body: If finiteness is only needed inside the proof, do not require a `Fintype` instance in the theorem statement. Introduce stronger finiteness data locally only when needed.

title: Prefer explicit proof terms for proof arguments
body:
When a function expects a proof, pass an actual proof term. Use `by decide` only when the proposition is decidable and computation is the intended proof method.
```lean
have h : 1 < a.size := by decide
exact f a h
```

title: Do not modify hypotheses in place unless necessary
body: Prefer deriving new facts from existing hypotheses rather than rewriting or mutating the originals. Keeping the original hypotheses intact usually makes the proof state easier to follow.

title: Prefer `rw`/`simp` over `▸`, and avoid `erw`
body: Use ordinary rewriting tactics rather than dependent rewrite syntax `▸` unless it is clearly the best option. Replace `erw` with proper rewrites, `change`, or better lemmas before merging.

title: Prefer canonical numeral and arithmetic formulations
body: Avoid brittle formulations such as `Nat.sub` and non-canonical numeral syntax like `bit0`/`bit1` in statements when a cleaner equivalent form exists. For naturals, prefer `n ≠ 0` to express nonzeroness, converting to `0 < n` only when needed.

title: Introduce binders directly in local facts when natural
body:
When a local fact is naturally quantified, write the binders in the `have` itself.
```lean
have h (x : α) (hx : x ∈ s) : P x := by ...
```

title: Use `gcongr` for monotonicity and congruence inequality goals
body: When an inequality is obtained by applying monotone operations to known inequalities, prefer `gcongr` over manually chaining low-level monotonicity lemmas.

title: Prefer equivalences for cardinality equalities
body: When proving two finite types or sets have the same cardinality, construct an equivalence and use `Fintype.card_congr` or `Nat.card_congr`.

title: Use `rintro` for structured introductions
body: When introducing hypotheses with patterns, prefer `rintro` over repeated `intro` followed by manual destructuring.

title: Revert dependent hypotheses together
body: If one hypothesis depends on another, revert the main hypothesis so dependent hypotheses are reverted with it, then re-introduce them after rewriting.

title: Prefer weaker natural assumptions in analytic and measure-theoretic statements
body: Do not assume full measurability, integrability, or similarly strong hypotheses if weaker standard assumptions such as `AEMeasurable` or local versions suffice.

title: Avoid asserting equality of typeclass instances
body: Do not prove equalities between instances unless absolutely necessary. Prove the mathematical property you need instead.

title: Use `simp only [...]` when exact control matters
body: When proof stability, performance, or intent depends on exactly which rewrites fire, use `simp only [...]` with a minimal lemma list. This is especially appropriate for non-terminal simplification.

title: Avoid non-terminal `simp`
body: Do not use broad `simp` in the middle of a proof when it leaves substantial work behind. For non-terminal simplification, prefer `simp only [...]`, `rw`, `simp_rw`, or `dsimp`; an exception is `simp` immediately followed by a normalizing tactic such as `ring`, `abel`, `omega`, or `norm_num`.

title: Prefer terminal `simp`
body: Use plain `simp` or `simp [...]` when it closes the goal. Only replace it with `simp only [...]` if you need tighter control for stability, performance, or debugging.

title: Only add `@[simp]` when it improves automation
body: Do not mark a lemma `@[simp]` just because it is easy to prove. A simp lemma should be broadly useful, should fire often, and should simplify toward a canonical form.

title: Mark genuinely useful canonical lemmas with `@[simp]`
body:
Add `@[simp]` to canonical evaluation, coercion, projection, constructor, and membership lemmas that users should rewrite with often. If the proof is definitional, prove it by `rfl`.
```lean
@[simp] theorem foo_apply (x) : foo x = ... := rfl
```

title: Orient simp lemmas toward canonical normal forms
body: State `@[simp]` lemmas with the more complicated or less canonical expression on the left and the simpler normal form on the right. For `iff` lemmas, put the more complicated condition on the left.

title: Prefer existing API lemmas over unfolding definitions
body: If Mathlib already has a lemma expressing the behavior you need, use it with `rw` or `simp` rather than unfolding the definition. This keeps proofs stable under refactors.

title: Prefer `@[simps]` to generate projection simp lemmas
body: When defining a structure-valued object, equivalence, or homomorphism, use `@[simps]` so Mathlib generates the standard projection and evaluation lemmas automatically instead of writing boilerplate by hand.

title: Inspect and control generated `@[simps]` lemmas
body: Check generated lemmas with `simps?` or `simps!?`, restrict to useful projections, and avoid generating noisy or unhelpful lemmas. If `@[simps]` produces the wrong shape, write the needed simp lemmas manually instead.

title: Do not add redundant simp lemmas
body: If `simp` can already prove the lemma from existing simp rules, or an existing simp lemma already performs the same rewrite, do not add another `[simp]` theorem. Keep the simp set minimal.

title: Prefer `simp` over long chains of simp-lemma rewrites
body: If a proof is mostly a sequence of rewrites by simp lemmas, replace it with one `simp` or `simpa` call. Conversely, if a single `rw` suffices, do not use `simp`.

title: Avoid `@[simp]` lemmas with hard side conditions
body: Do not mark lemmas `[simp]` if they require hypotheses that `simp` usually cannot infer or discharge cheaply. Prefer formulations whose assumptions are easy for typeclass inference or `simp` to handle.

title: Prefer dedicated coercion simp lemmas over relying on defeq
body: If `simp` does not rewrite through a coercion by definitional equality alone, add a targeted simp lemma matching the exact coercion or application pattern. Do not rely on fragile unfolding behavior.

title: Prefer `simpa ... using ...` for direct simplification
body:
When a goal is solved by simplifying an existing term or hypothesis, write `simpa [...] using h` rather than `simp [...] at h; exact h` or `simp [...]; exact h`.
```lean
simpa [foo, bar] using h
```

title: Ensure a simp lemma can actually fire
body: A simp lemma should have enough information on its left-hand side for `simp` to infer the needed parameters. Avoid underdetermined lemmas whose arguments cannot be reconstructed from the target expression.

title: Keep simp lemma left-hand sides in simp-normal form
body: Do not mark a lemma `[simp]` if its left-hand side itself simplifies further by existing simp lemmas. Restate the lemma in the form `simp` actually sees instead of suppressing the `simpNF` linter.

title: Avoid simp loops and expanding rewrites
body: Do not add `[simp]` lemmas that rewrite back and forth, recreate their own left-hand side, or make expressions larger. In particular, avoid non-affine rewrites where variables occur more often on the RHS than on the LHS.

title: Use explicit arguments when inference is fragile
body:
If `simp` or `rw` fails because implicit parameters, typeclass arguments, or specialization are not inferred well, specify the important arguments explicitly.
```lean
simp [lemma (a := x)]
rw [@one_mul α _]
```

title: Avoid `simp_all` unless necessary
body: `simp_all` is aggressive and rewrites the whole context. Prefer targeted `simp at h ⊢` or `simp only [...]` unless you genuinely need hypotheses and goal to simplify each other.

title: Unfold only enough to expose the right rewrite
body: If `simp` does not fire because the target is hidden behind a reducible definition or partial application, first `dsimp` or unfold just enough to expose the pattern, then simplify. Avoid unnecessary unfolding.

title: Add small reusable simp lemmas instead of repeating local rewrites
body: If many proofs need the same tiny rewrite, add the corresponding simp lemma near the definition rather than repeatedly introducing local helper facts.

title: Use `@[reassoc (attr := simp)]` for categorical composition lemmas
body: For category-theoretic lemmas meant to rewrite under composition, use `@[reassoc (attr := simp)]` so both the original and reassociated forms are available to `simp`.

title: Use `@[simps!]` when stronger unfolding is needed
body: If ordinary `@[simps]` does not see through the definition enough to generate useful lemmas, use `@[simps!]`, especially for non-constructor bodies or type synonyms.

title: Treat `simpNF` linter failures as real issues
body: If the `simpNF` linter reports a problem, fix the lemma statement or attribute rather than silencing it with `nolint simpNF`, except as a documented last resort.

title: Avoid `erw`
body: Do not use `erw`. If `rw` fails, normalize with `dsimp`, `change`, or `simp only`, or add the proper API lemma so ordinary rewriting works.

title: Keep `dsimp` targeted
body: Prefer `dsimp only [...]` or `dsimp at h` over broad `dsimp at *`. Uncontrolled definitional unfolding is noisy, fragile, and often makes goals larger.

title: Do not expose implementation details in simp normal forms
body: Simp should rewrite toward the public API, not toward internal fields, recursors, `.1`/`.2`, or low-level representations. Avoid simp lemmas that break abstraction barriers.

title: Provide both forward and inverse simp lemmas for equivalences when canonical
body:
For `Equiv`, `MulEquiv`, isomorphisms, and similar objects intended for rewriting, provide simp lemmas for both directions when both are canonical and non-looping.
```lean
@[simp] theorem e_apply_symm_apply ...
@[simp] theorem e_symm_apply_apply ...
```

title: Use dedicated automation for function properties
body: For continuity, measurability, and similar goals, use the dedicated automation such as `fun_prop` and measurability tactics, and tag supporting lemmas with the appropriate attributes rather than trying to solve everything with `simp`.

title: Keep simp attributes consistent across related lemmas
body: If one lemma in a natural family is `[simp]`, analogous variants usually should be too—or none should be. Inconsistent simp tagging across parallel APIs is confusing.

title: Use local simp attributes for narrowly useful rules
body: If a simp rule is useful only in one proof, section, or file, use `attribute [local simp] ...` or `@[scoped simp]` rather than making it global.

title: Do not use `simp` as a substitute for arithmetic automation
body: Use `norm_num`, `omega`, `linarith`, `positivity`, `ring`, `ring_nf`, `field_simp`, and similar tactics for numeric or algebraic side conditions. Let `simp` expose the arithmetic structure, then use the dedicated tactic.

title: Pass local hypotheses explicitly to `simp`
body:
If simplification depends on local facts, include them explicitly with `simp [h]`, `simp only [h]`, or `simp [h] at hx ⊢`. Do not rely on `simp` to guess the right local hypothesis.
```lean
simp [h, foo] at hx ⊢
```

title: Do not tag definitions themselves with `@[simp]`
body: Put `@[simp]` on lemmas about a definition, not on the `def` or `abbrev` itself, unless global unfolding is truly intended. For structures and maps, prefer `@[simps]` or `@[simps!]` to generate projection lemmas.

title: Rewrite hypotheses and goals precisely
body: When the same rewrite should apply to a hypothesis and the goal, write `simp only [...] at h ⊢` or `rw [...] at h ⊢`. Avoid `at *`, which may rewrite unrelated hypotheses.

title: Prefer `dsimp` for pure definitional reduction
body: Use `dsimp`/`dsimp only` when you only need definitional unfolding such as beta, zeta, iota, or projection reduction. Use `simp` when you want rewriting by simp lemmas.

title: Use `to_additive` with simp attributes explicitly
body:
When a lemma’s simp behavior should transfer to its additive version, use the supported form
```lean
@[to_additive (attr := simp)]
```
and verify that the generated declaration gets the intended attributes.

title: Configure simps projections after coercion setup
body:
For custom structures, register the intended projections with `initialize_simps_projections` after the relevant `FunLike`/`CoeFun` instance is in place, so generated lemmas use the right names and shapes.
```lean
initialize_simps_projections MyStruct (toFun → apply)
```

title: Prove definitional equalities by `rfl`
body: If a lemma is definitionally true, write it as `:= rfl` rather than `by simp` or usually even `by rfl`. This makes the lemma usable by `dsimp` and clearly signals definitional equality.

title: Do not make algebraic rearrangement lemmas global simp rules
body: Do not mark commutativity, associativity, distributivity, or similar rearrangement lemmas as global `[simp]`. Use them explicitly with `rw`, `simp_rw`, or local `simp only [...]` when needed.

title: Disable problematic simp rules locally
body:
If a generally good simp lemma is undesirable in one proof, remove it locally with `simp [-lemma]` or local attribute changes. Do not weaken the global simp set for an isolated case.
```lean
simp [-bad_lemma]
```

title: Remove redundant simplification
body: Delete `simp`, `simp only`, `rw`, or `dsimp` calls that do not materially change the proof state, including rewrites that merely repeat what a previous `simp` already did.

title: Use `@[norm_cast]` only for genuine cast-normalization lemmas
body: Add `@[norm_cast]` only to lemmas that normalize casts, with an explicit coercion on the left-hand side. Prefer the `norm_cast`/`push_cast`/`pull_cast` framework over ad hoc simp lemmas for coercions.

title: Prefer a new simp-normal-form copy over forcing a bad statement
body: If an existing theorem has a useful noncanonical statement, keep it, and add a separate simp-normal-form version tagged `@[simp]`. Do not force a noncanonical theorem into the simp set.

title: Use `simp_rw` for repeated or under-binder rewriting
body:
Use `simp_rw [...]` when you need repeated directed rewriting, especially under binders or throughout a term. Prefer it over repeated `rw` for rewrites inside `∀`, `∃`, `iSup`, and similar expressions.
```lean
simp_rw [sub_eq_add_neg, map_add]
```

title: Prefer `rfl` for trivial definitional goals
body: When the goal is exactly a definitional equality, use `rfl` rather than a gratuitous `simp`, unless you are intentionally proving a simp lemma.

title: Register coercions properly for cast tools
body: If `norm_cast` or related tools must detect a coercion after unfolding, ensure the coercion is registered appropriately so cast normalization can see it.

title: Use `rw` for a single targeted rewrite
body:
If you want one specific rewrite in one place, use `rw`/`rw [...] at h` rather than `simp only [...]`. Reserve `simp` for simplification, not one-off rewriting.
```lean
rw [isNClique_induce_iff] at h
```

title: Add simp-friendly membership and predicate characterization lemmas
body: For new predicates and set-like constructions, provide canonical lemmas of the form `x ∈ S ↔ P x` or `foo x ↔ ...`, and mark them `[simp]` when they are the standard way to reason about the predicate.

title: Simplify before arithmetic normalization
body: Run targeted `simp` first to rewrite definitions and obvious identities, then call `ring_nf`, `norm_num`, `field_simp`, `linarith`, or similar tactics. This keeps arithmetic automation focused on actual arithmetic.

title: Avoid coercion simp lemmas that recurse or fight cast normalization
body: A coercion simp lemma should strictly simplify and should not reintroduce the same coercion pattern on the RHS. Also avoid simp lemmas that conflict with `norm_cast`, `push_cast`, or `pull_cast`.

title: Use contextual `simp` deliberately
body: When simplification must use hypotheses introduced inside the goal, enable contextual simplification explicitly with `simp +contextual`, `simp [*]`, or the corresponding config. Do not rely on contextual rewriting by accident.

title: Prefer the strongest general lemma as the simp lemma
body: When several versions of a result exist, put `[simp]` or related automation attributes on the most general useful version rather than on weaker special cases.

title: Use `FunLike` coercions as the canonical form
body: For morphism-like structures, use the standard `FunLike`/`CoeFun` machinery so elaborated terms have stable heads and existing simp lemmas apply uniformly.

title: Prefer `iff` lemmas for rewrite-friendly logical characterizations
body: When a result is meant to be used by rewriting, state it as an `↔` rather than a one-way implication. This makes it usable by both `rw` and `simp`.

title: Combine adjacent simplification steps
body: Merge consecutive `simp`, `simp only`, `rw`, or `dsimp` steps on the same goal when one call can do the job. Repeated tiny cleanup steps add noise and are harder to maintain.

title: Use `@[reassoc]` without `simp` when only reassociation is wanted
body: If a categorical lemma should support reassociation rewriting but should not be a global simp rule, use `@[reassoc]` alone.

title: Add `@[ext]` lemmas for structures commonly proved equal by components
body: If equality proofs repeatedly reduce to equality of underlying data, provide an extensionality lemma so users can write `ext; simp` instead of manual field-by-field proofs.

title: Give simp lemmas a stable constant head
body: The left-hand side of a simp lemma should have a stable constant head so the simplifier can index it effectively. Avoid variable-headed, lambda-headed, metavariable-headed, or overly broad patterns.

title: Prefer `ext; simp` for equality of maps and structures
body:
When proving equality of morphisms, linear maps, natural transformations, and similar objects, prefer extensionality followed by simplification when the simp API supports it.
```lean
ext x <;> simp
```

title: Avoid overlapping or competing simp lemmas
body: If two simp lemmas cover essentially the same pattern and one subsumes the other, keep only the stronger or more general one as `[simp]`. Competing simp lemmas make behavior less predictable and can hurt performance.

title: Provide evaluation and coercion simp lemmas for function-like objects
body:
For maps, homs, embeddings, equivalences, and wrappers, add the evaluation lemmas users actually need, typically `foo_apply`, `map_apply`, `hom_apply`, or coercion lemmas.
```lean
@[simp] theorem coe_mk : ⇑(mk f h) = f := rfl
@[simp] theorem map_apply (x) : map f x = ... := rfl
```

title: Use `field_simp` only in its proper role
body: Use `field_simp` to clear denominators in expressions involving division or inverses, typically near the end of a proof and often followed by `ring`. Do not treat `field_simp` as a general replacement for `simp`.

title: Keep simp lemmas compatible with `field_simp`
body: Do not add global simp lemmas that interfere with denominator normalization or the side conditions generated by `field_simp`. Rewrite hypotheses into the forms `field_simp` expects before using it.

title: Be conservative about changing the global simp set
body: Do not add or remove global simp attributes casually, especially in unrelated refactors or ports. Changes to global simp behavior should be deliberate, justified, and reviewed carefully.

title: Avoid redundant evaluation simp lemmas
body: Do not add extra `_apply_apply`-style simp lemmas if they are already derivable from a simpler `_apply` lemma. Keep the simp API minimal.

title: Do not make broad functor equalities global simp lemmas
body: Avoid putting equalities of functors into the global simp set. Prefer object-level or morphism-level simp lemmas, or use isomorphism-based APIs instead.

title: Prefer canonical notation and normal forms in statements
body: Write lemmas using standard Mathlib forms, such as `<`/`≤` instead of `>`/`≥`, and other canonical spellings that match existing simp lemmas. This improves rewriting and reduces duplicate API.

title: Formulate propositional and boolean simp lemmas in direct rewrite form
body: For propositions, prefer `P ↔ True`, `P ↔ False`, or other direct `iff` forms rather than `P = True`. For booleans, use canonical boolean or logical forms rather than burying `b = true` or `b = false` inside larger patterns.

title: Use `simp?` and tracing to debug `simp`
body:
If a `simp` call is fragile, slow, or surprising, run `simp?` to discover a good lemma list, and use tracing when needed to inspect fired rewrites.
```lean
set_option trace.Meta.Tactic.simp.rewrite true
```
Replace exploratory calls with explicit `simp only [...]` once you know what is needed.

title: Use public coercion syntax in simp lemmas
body: State coercion and evaluation lemmas using public syntax such as `⇑f` and `(x : α)`, not internal fields like `toFun` or `.1`, unless those are the stable public API.

title: Use `simp [h]` after `by_cases`
body: After `by_cases h : P`, simplify branch goals with `simp [h]`. Avoid unnecessary extra cleanup such as `simp at h` unless it is actually needed.

title: Match simp lemmas to the expression shape users actually see
body:
State simp lemmas in the fully applied form that appears in goals, especially for projections and coercions. If simplification fails on an application shape, add a lemma for that exact shape.
```lean
@[simp] theorem Equiv.refl_apply (a : α) : Equiv.refl α a = a := rfl
```

title: Use `.not` on existing iff lemmas for negated rewrites
body: When rewriting a negated equivalence, prefer the `.not` form of an existing `iff` lemma rather than introducing separate `not_*` lemmas or manual `push_neg` boilerplate.

title: Use `subst` for variable substitution
body: When you have an equality of the form `x = t` or `t = x` and want to replace a variable everywhere, prefer `subst x` over forcing `simp` or repeated `rw`.

title: Do not use implications as simp rules
body: Simp works best with equalities and `iff` lemmas. Do not try to use plain implications as simp rules.

title: Avoid broad `simp` steps in `calc` blocks
body: In `calc` proofs, prefer `rw`, `ring`, or another precise justification over a broad `simp` step. If `simp` is necessary, use `simp only [...]` to keep the step robust.

title: Keep automation focused and curated
body: When using tactics like `grind` or `apply_rules`, supply only the lemmas they actually need, for example with `only [...]`. Do not stack heavy tactics as a catch-all.

title: Prefer `refine` for partial constructions and controlled subgoals
body:
Use `refine` when you want to build part of a term now and leave explicit holes for later. Prefer `?_` placeholders for goal-generating holes, not `_`.
```lean
refine ⟨x, hx, ?_⟩
```

title: Use controlled simplification when stability matters
body:
Use `simp only [...]` or `dsimp only [...]` when you want a specific simplification set or controlled definitional unfolding. Avoid broad nonterminal `simp` calls that obscure which lemmas are doing the work.
```lean
simp only [map_mul, mul_assoc]
dsimp only [foo, bar]
```

title: Use explicit or named arguments when inference is brittle
body:
When applying lemmas with many arguments or ambiguous implicits, provide explicit or named arguments to stabilize elaboration and avoid dependence on argument order.
```lean
rw [lemma_name (j := j) (j' := j')]
```

title: Close solved goals with `exact`
body:
Use `exact` when you already have a term of the goal type. Do not use `apply` or `refine` as stylistic substitutes when no subgoals are intended.
```lean
exact h
```

title: Keep tactic layout readable
body: Prefer one tactic per line unless the whole proof is a short one-liner. Lean tactic blocks are indentation-sensitive, so preserve clear nesting and grouping.

title: Prefer short term-style proofs over tactic blocks
body:
If a proof is naturally a single term, write it directly instead of opening tactic mode. Avoid wrappers like `by exact` or `by apply`.
```lean
def foo : ∃ x, p x := ⟨x, hx⟩
```

title: Keep rewriting and simplification local and goal-directed
body: Rewrite only the subexpressions you need, when you need them. Avoid large premature rewrites of the goal or hypotheses that make later steps harder to understand.

title: Combine consecutive rewrites
body:
When several rewrites happen in the same proof state, combine them into one `rw [...]` call. This reduces noise and makes the proof easier to read.
```lean
rw [h1, h2, ← h3]
```

title: Add automation attributes deliberately
body: Tag lemmas with attributes such as `@[simp]`, `@[ext]`, `@[gcongr]`, `@[fun_prop]`, `@[mono]`, `@[norm_cast]`, `@[aesop]`, or `@[grind]` only when they are genuinely intended for that automation. Avoid tags that create loops, ambiguity, or performance regressions.

title: Reuse existing tactic infrastructure in metaprograms
body: Before adding a new tactic, check whether core Lean or Mathlib already provides the needed behavior. Prefer thin wrappers around existing tactics and APIs over duplicating logic.

title: Use tracing and inspection tools for debugging, not final code
body: When debugging tactics or inference, use tracing options, simp traces, definitional-equality traces, profiler output, or `show_term` rather than guessing. Remove debugging options and scaffolding from committed code.

title: Prefer `simpa` when simplification closes the goal
body:
Use `simpa` or `simpa using h` when a hypothesis or lemma solves the goal up to simplification. Do not use `simpa` if no simplification is actually needed.
```lean
simpa [foo] using h
```

title: Prefer automation-friendly API lemmas over repeated unfolding
body: If a proof pattern recurs, add a dedicated simp lemma, ext lemma, congruence lemma, or other API lemma instead of repeatedly unfolding definitions by hand.

title: Prefer `let` for data and `have` for propositions
body: Use `let` for non-`Prop` local definitions and `have` for local facts. Use `letI` or `haveI` only when introducing local instances for typeclass inference.

title: Prefer the right normalization tactic for the domain
body: Use specialized tactics such as `ring`, `ring_nf`, `abel`, `field_simp`, `field`, `linarith`, `nlinarith`, `norm_num`, `norm_cast`, `mod_cast`, `push_cast`, `lift`, `zify`, `qify`, or `linear_combination` when they match the goal. Do not replace a clean specialized proof with broad automation.

title: Avoid repeated expensive elaboration or inference in tactic code
body: Do not repeatedly elaborate the same syntax or synthesize the same instances inside loops. Cache results and provide explicit arguments when inference is slow or ambiguous.

title: Prefer the canonical Lean 4 / Mathlib tactic
body: Use the standard, non-legacy tactic or syntax for the job. Avoid deprecated or duplicate variants such as `cases'`, `induction'`, `split`, `existsi`, `refine'`, or `native_decide` unless there is a specific technical reason.

title: Use `erw` only as a last resort
body: Prefer `rw`, possibly after `dsimp` or `change`. Reach for `erw` only when ordinary rewriting fails because of transparency or definitional-equality issues.

title: Prefer `obtain` for forward witness extraction in a single goal
body: When destructuring an existential or structured hypothesis without branching into multiple goals, prefer `obtain`. Use `rcases` more for genuine case splits.

title: Use `gcongr` for monotonicity-style relational goals
body: For inequalities and similar goals that follow from monotonicity or generalized congruence, prefer `gcongr` over manual monotonicity lemmas. Tag supporting lemmas with `@[gcongr]` when appropriate.

title: Use `rintro` to combine introduction and destructuring
body: When you immediately destruct introduced hypotheses, use `rintro` instead of `intro` followed by `rcases` or `cases`.

title: Keep one active goal at a time when possible
body: Structure proofs so side goals are handled promptly and locally instead of letting many unrelated goals accumulate.

title: Use goal combinators and subgoal blocks only when they clarify structure
body: Use `case` blocks for named goals and `<;>` only for genuinely uniform follow-up across all branches. Avoid `swap`, broad `all_goals`, or anonymous focus syntax when they make proof structure less explicit.

title: Avoid `convert` unless it clearly simplifies reuse
body:
Prefer direct rewriting or `simpa using` over `convert`. If `convert` is genuinely the clearest tool, use `convert ... using n` to control matching depth.
```lean
convert h using 2 <;> simp
```

title: Avoid `unfold` in proofs when API lemmas suffice
body: Prefer public rewrite lemmas, `rw`, `simp`, or `dsimp` over `unfold`. Use definitional unfolding only in a controlled way when no suitable API exists.

title: Prefer local `classical` and narrowly scoped options
body:
When classical reasoning or special options are needed, introduce them locally rather than globally. Use `classical`, `set_option ... in`, and local attributes only where needed.
```lean
by
  classical
  ...
```

title: Prefer `induction` over legacy induction variants
body: In Lean 4 / Mathlib code, use `induction`, not `induction'`. Prefer structured `induction ... with | ... => ...` syntax and `case` blocks.

title: Prefer compact constructor and existential syntax
body:
When building conjunctions, existentials, or structures, use `⟨..., ...⟩`, `use`, or `refine ⟨..., ?_⟩` rather than `constructor`/`existsi` boilerplate. Use `constructor` for constructor goals, not legacy `split`.
```lean
use 3
exact ⟨hx, hy⟩
```

title: Prefer structured destructuring tactics
body:
Use `rcases`, `obtain`, and `rintro` to unpack `∃`, `∧`, `∨`, sigma types, and constructor arguments. Avoid manual `cases` chains when structured destructuring is clearer.
```lean
rintro ⟨x, hx, hy⟩
obtain ⟨x, hx⟩ := h
```

title: Replace search tactics with explicit final code
body: Tactics such as `exact?`, `apply?`, and `simp?` are for discovery, not committed proof scripts. Replace them with the concrete lemma application or explicit simplification set they suggest.

title: Prefer `fun_prop` for routine function-property goals
body:
When the goal is continuity, measurability, differentiability, or a similar tagged function property, use `fun_prop`. Tag intended lemmas with `@[fun_prop]`.
```lean
by fun_prop
```

title: Use `lia` for linear arithmetic over `Nat` and `Int`
body: For linear arithmetic over naturals and integers, prefer `lia`. Use other arithmetic tactics only when the domain or goal shape calls for them.

title: Prefer `filter_upwards` for `∀ᶠ` and filter reasoning
body:
For `Eventually` goals and hypotheses, use `filter_upwards` rather than unpacking filter definitions manually. If a direct `.mono` proof suffices, that is also fine.
```lean
filter_upwards [h₁, h₂] with x hx₁ hx₂
```

title: Tactic implementations should make real progress and preserve goals correctly
body: Do not let a tactic report success while leaving the proof state unchanged. Manage the goal list explicitly and do not accidentally drop trailing goals; use APIs such as `replaceMainGoal` when appropriate.

title: Use `apply` only for backward reasoning that should create subgoals
body:
Use `apply` when the goal matches the conclusion of a lemma and you want its premises as new goals. Do not use `apply` as a generic replacement for `exact`.
```lean
apply le_trans
```

title: Use `norm_num` for concrete numerical goals
body: When the goal is straightforward arithmetic with concrete numerals, prefer `norm_num`. Use `by decide` only after simplification has reduced the goal to a small decidable proposition.

title: Avoid `change` unless definitional reshaping is genuinely the point
body: Do not use `change` as routine goal massaging. Prefer `rw`, `simp`, `simpa`, or a better-targeted lemma unless you specifically need a definitionally equal target.

title: Use `#guard_msgs` and related tools for tactic tests
body: When testing tactics, elaborators, warnings, or expected failures, use `#guard_msgs`, `fail_if_success`, and similar tools rather than unchecked comments or informal examples.

title: Prefer specialized automation over broad automation
body: When a specialized tactic matches the domain, use it instead of broad search tactics like `aesop`, `grind`, `library_search`, `exact?`, or `apply?`. Specialized automation is usually clearer, faster, and more stable.

title: Use heavy automation sparingly and usually terminally
body: Avoid search tactics when a short direct proof is clearer. If you use them, prefer terminal use or tightly controlled rule sets/configuration.

title: Prefer `by_cases!`, `by_contra!`, and `contrapose!` when pushing negations helps
body:
Use the `!` variants when you want negations pushed automatically through hypotheses or goals. For ordinary proposition splits, use `by_cases h : P`.
```lean
by_contra! h
by_cases! h : x = 0
```

title: In meta code, manage context and reduction explicitly
body: Use `withMainContext` when interacting with the local context, but do not confuse it with goal focusing. Normalize expressions with `whnf`/`whnfR`/`whnfD` before shape-based inspection when reduction is relevant.

title: Prefer `ext` for extensionality goals
body:
When proving equality of functions, sets, or other extensional objects, use `ext` to reduce to pointwise goals. Name variables explicitly when helpful, and use depth bounds like `ext : 1` if needed.
```lean
ext x
simp [h]
```

title: Use `ring` and `abel` near the end of normalization proofs
body: Use `ring`/`ring_nf` for polynomial identities and `abel`/`abel_nf` for additive commutative-group normalization, after the expression is in the right form. Do not use them as early catch-all steps.

title: Keep theorem statements and local contexts minimal
body: Do not introduce unnecessary variables, hypotheses, or local instances. Extra context makes elaboration, automation, and case splits harder to control.

title: In metaprograms, prefer macros for syntax sugar and `MetaM` for real logic
body: If custom syntax is only a wrapper around existing syntax, implement it with `macro_rules`. Put substantive proof-search or transformation logic in `MetaM` over `MVarId`s, and keep `TacticM` as a thin interactive wrapper.

title: Prefer dedicated quotient, closure, or domain-specific induction principles
body: For quotients, closures, or other generated predicates, use the dedicated induction or elimination principles provided by the API rather than generic `cases`.

title: Use `positivity` for positivity and nonnegativity side goals
body:
When the goal is of the form `0 ≤ _` or `0 < _`, especially after `gcongr` or algebraic preprocessing, try `positivity` first.
```lean
positivity
```

title: Prefer `inferInstance` or `inferInstanceAs` over manual instance construction
body:
If typeclass inference can synthesize the instance, let it do so. Use `inferInstanceAs` when you want to specify the target type explicitly.
```lean
exact inferInstance
```

title: Prefer `rfl` for definitional equalities
body:
If the goal is definitionally true, close it with `rfl` rather than heavier automation. Also use `rfl` patterns in destructuring when they eliminate equalities immediately.
```lean
obtain ⟨x, rfl⟩ := h
```

title: Prefer `congr!` for congruence-style subexpression changes
body:
Use `congr!` to rewrite inside larger expressions or strip matching outer structure. Use plain `congr` only when you specifically need it.
```lean
congr! 1
```

title: Prefer `obtain rfl | h := eq_or_ne ...` for equality splits
body:
When splitting on whether a term equals a distinguished value, use `obtain rfl | h := eq_or_ne ...` so the `rfl` branch rewrites automatically.
```lean
obtain rfl | hx := eq_or_ne x 0
```

title: Prefer `decide` or `by decide` over `native_decide`
body: In Mathlib, use `decide` or `by decide` for decidable goals. Do not commit `native_decide` proofs.

title: Use `conv` only for genuinely targeted rewriting
body: If ordinary rewriting is insufficient, use `conv` to target a specific occurrence or position. Make the target occurrence unambiguous rather than using `conv` as a routine rewrite tool.

title: Use `field_simp` or `field` only when denominators are the issue
body: Use `field_simp` as preprocessing to clear denominators once the goal is in the right shape, and provide the needed nonzero side conditions explicitly. Do not expect `field_simp` or `field` to solve the whole proof.

title: Use cast-normalization tactics for coercion cleanup
body:
When casts between numeric types are the main obstacle, use `norm_cast`, `mod_cast`, or `push_cast` rather than manual rewriting.
```lean
norm_cast
```

title: Avoid unnecessary explicit instance arguments
body: Do not thread named instance arguments through proofs unless needed for disambiguation, performance, or elaboration stability.

title: Use `rwa` only when the combined rewrite-and-close step is clearly better
body:
If a rewrite immediately turns the goal or hypothesis into something solved, `rwa` can be concise. Otherwise prefer explicit `rw`/`simp` plus `exact` or `assumption`.
```lean
rwa [h] at hx
```

title: Prefer `calc` for readable chains
body:
When a proof is naturally a chain of equalities or inequalities, use `calc` rather than repeated transitivity lemmas or a long tactic script.
```lean
calc
  a = b := by rw [h]
  _ ≤ c := hc
```

title: Use `generalizing` or specialized induction principles when needed
body:
If the induction hypothesis must apply to extra variables, use `generalizing` rather than revert/reintroduce boilerplate. If there is a natural specialized induction principle, use `induction ... using ...`.
```lean
induction n generalizing x with
```

title: Use `subst` or `obtain rfl` for variable equalities
body:
When an equality identifies a variable with a term, rewrite it away globally with `subst` or `obtain rfl := h` rather than carrying the equality around.
```lean
subst x
```

title: Prefer `choose` for uniform existential witnesses
body: When a hypothesis has the form `∀ x, ∃ y, P x y`, use `choose` instead of manually chaining `Exists.choose`.

title: Use `wlog` only for genuine without-loss-of-generality arguments
body: Use `wlog` or `wlog!` only when one case truly reduces to another by symmetry or normalization. For independent branches, use `by_cases` instead.

title: Use `simp_rw` for repeated rewriting or rewriting under binders
body:
Prefer `simp_rw` when the same rewrite should happen repeatedly or inside binders, lambdas, or quantifiers. It is often clearer and more robust than many repeated `rw` calls.
```lean
simp_rw [foo, ← bar]
```

title: Use `split_ifs` for `if`-expressions
body:
When the proof naturally splits on an `if`, use `split_ifs with h` and solve each branch with the resulting hypotheses.
```lean
split_ifs with h <;> simp [h]
```

title: Prefer `cases` over `induction` when no induction hypothesis is needed
body: If you only need constructor splitting and no recursive hypothesis, use `cases`, not `induction`.

title: Use `nontriviality` for `Nontrivial` assumptions
body: When a proof needs `[Nontrivial α]`, use `nontriviality α` early rather than manually constructing the instance. Tag supporting lemmas with `@[nontriviality]` when appropriate.

title: Prefer existing filter, asymptotic, and domain combinators over rebuilding proofs
body: For notions such as `Tendsto`, `IsBigO`, `IsTheta`, and similar APIs, use the library combinators and standard lemmas rather than reproving the underlying machinery from scratch.

title: Prefer direct application of hypotheses and iff projections
body: If you need `H x hx`, write that directly rather than `specialize H x hx`. For `↔` lemmas, use `.mp` and `.mpr` when they make the proof shorter and clearer.

title: Use dedicated domain tactics where they are standard
body: For specialized domains, prefer the tactics that encode the standard workflow, such as `cat_disch`, `aesop_cat`, `tfae_have`, `tfae_finish`, `coherence`, `monoidal`, `fin_cases`, or `interval_cases`, when they fit the goal.

title: Prefer `@[simps]` and `simps` for projection lemmas
body: When defining structures, equivalences, or morphisms, use `@[simps]` or related projection-generation support rather than hand-writing routine projection lemmas, unless the generated lemmas are unsuitable.

title: Prefer `apply_fun` for transporting equalities through functions
body: When you want to apply a function to both sides of an equality or equality hypothesis, use `apply_fun`. This is clearer than manually invoking congruence lemmas.

title: Prefer direct projections when destructuring is unnecessary
body: If you only need fields like `h.1` and `h.2`, use them directly instead of destructuring the whole hypothesis.

title: Preserve source positions and good error messages in macros
body: Macro expansions should keep errors pointing at the relevant user syntax. Do not obscure failures by losing source locations during expansion.

title: Prefer `let` over `set` unless you need the defining equation
body: Use `let` for a local abbreviation when you do not need an equation in the context. Use `set x := expr with hx` only when the equation `hx` will be used later.

title: Make inferable arguments implicit and non-inferable ones explicit
body: Use implicit binders `{...}` for parameters Lean can reliably infer from explicit arguments or the goal. Keep central or non-inferable parameters explicit, and when specifying implicits manually, prefer named arguments like `(R := R)` over `@foo _ _ _`.

title: Reuse surrounding binders, but keep declaration signatures explicit
body: Do not redeclare parameters or typeclass assumptions already in scope, and factor shared binders into a local `variable` block or `section` when several nearby declarations use them. Still write each declaration with the binders you want it to depend on; do not rely on ambient variables leaking in implicitly.

title: Keep top-level layout and indentation standard
body: Start top-level commands at column 0. Indent proof bodies by 2 spaces and wrapped declarations or continuations in standard Mathlib style.

title: Open namespaces and scopes sparingly
body: Keep `open`, `open scoped`, and local notation as narrow as practical. Prefer qualified names or `open ... in` when that avoids file-wide namespace pollution.

title: Use dot notation when it improves readability
body: Prefer forms like `h.symm`, `x.foo`, or `f.comp g` when they make code shorter and clearer. Avoid dot notation when name resolution becomes ambiguous or fragile.

title: Use standard spacing and avoid manual alignment
body: Write `x : α`, `a + b`, `rw [h₁, h₂]`, and similar standard spacing. Do not align columns with extra spaces; use ordinary indentation so diffs stay stable.

title: Parenthesize only when helpful
body: Remove unnecessary parentheses around simple expressions, but add them whenever precedence or parsing could be unclear, especially with custom notation, coercions, arrows, or unary operators.

title: Keep contexts minimal
body: Remove unused parameters, hypotheses, universes, local `have`/`let` bindings, and obsolete helpers. If a binder is intentionally unused, write `_` or a clearly unused name like `_h`.

title: Reuse existing Mathlib APIs and notation
body: Do not reimplement standard lemmas, wrappers, encodings, or notation that already exist in Mathlib/Core. Prefer canonical forms such as `x ∈ s`, `∅`, `Set.univ`, `a * b`, `n + 1`, and standard bundled structures.

title: Put parameters and hypotheses in binder form before the colon
body: Prefer declaration headers like `lemma foo (x : α) (h : P x) : Q x := ...` rather than encoding assumptions with a leading `∀` or trailing implications when ordinary binder syntax works better.

title: Use conventional Mathlib names
body: Choose standard names such as `α β`, `R`, `G`, `x y`, `s t`, `i j`, and hypothesis names like `h`, `h₁`, `h₂`. Match nearby files’ naming and argument conventions rather than inventing a new local style.

title: Inline one-use helpers; name repeated nontrivial expressions
body: If a short local helper is used only once, inline it. If a nontrivial expression or fact is used several times, give it a local name and reuse it consistently.

title: Do not use broad option hacks to force proofs through
body: Do not use escapes like `set_option maxHeartbeats 0` to bypass performance problems. If an option change is unavoidable, keep it local, conservative, and justified.

title: Localize options and lint suppressions
body: Use `set_option ... in` and other suppressions on the smallest possible region. Fix the underlying issue rather than disabling linters or adding `@[nolint]` unless the exception is deliberate and justified.

title: Prefer `fun x ↦ ...` for lambdas
body:
In Mathlib code, write lambda expressions with `↦`. Reserve `=>` for match arms and other standard syntax roles.
```lean
def f : ℕ → ℕ := fun x ↦ x + 1
```

title: Do not abuse definitional equality or implementation details
body: Do not rely on fragile defeq coincidences between wrappers, coercions, or structures, and do not use low-level projections or fields when the intended API provides named lemmas or notation. Prefer extensionality lemmas, membership notation, and named projections over implementation-specific details.

title: Drop redundant namespace qualifiers
body: If a namespace is already open or you are already inside it, omit unnecessary qualification. Keep qualification only when it helps disambiguate.

title: Keep `autoImplicit` off and declare variables explicitly
body: Do not rely on undeclared implicit variables. Mathlib convention is to declare all variables explicitly.

title: Use `let` for data, `have` for propositions, and `letI`/`haveI` for instances
body: Keep the distinction between data, facts, and instances clear. Use `let` for values, `have` for propositions, and `letI` or `haveI` only for local instances.

title: Keep `:=`, `by`, and infix operators at the end of the previous line
body: Do not start a new line with `:`, `:=`, `by`, or infix operators such as `=`, `+`, `*`, `≫`, or `∘`. Break lines so these tokens stay attached to the preceding line.

title: Scope `noncomputable` and classical reasoning locally
body: If code is noncomputable, mark only the relevant declaration or use `noncomputable section` only when many nearby declarations need it. Prefer `classical`, `by classical`, or `open scoped Classical in` over global `open Classical`.

title: Use `def`, `lemma`, `theorem`, `abbrev`, and `instance` appropriately
body:
Use `def` for data, `lemma`/`theorem` for propositions, and `instance` for typeclass instances rather than `@[instance] theorem`. Use `abbrev` only for transparent aliases that should remain reducible.
```lean
instance : Foo α := ...
abbrev MyNat := Nat
```

title: Use standard coercion notation and avoid unnecessary coercions
body: When an explicit coercion is needed, write `↑x` or `⇑f`, not raw coercion constants. Omit explicit coercions when Lean can insert them unambiguously, but add them when the intended type would otherwise be unclear.

title: Name intermediate facts explicitly
body:
Give meaningful names to important `have`, `obtain`, and similar intermediate results rather than relying on `this` or autogenerated names.
```lean
have hmul : a * b = b * a := ...
```

title: Avoid unnecessary `@` applications and underscore-heavy argument lists
body: Let Lean infer implicit arguments when it can. If you must specify them, prefer named arguments over `@foo _ _ _`.

title: Follow the surrounding file’s conventions
body: Match the local file’s established naming, notation, formatting, binder style, and proof style unless there is a clear reason not to. Do not make unrelated style-only rewrites in the same PR.

title: Use `where` blocks and named fields for structures and instances
body:
Prefer `where` syntax and named fields over anonymous constructors or positional structure syntax, especially for multi-field structures.
```lean
instance : Foo α where
  bar := ...
  baz := ...
```

title: Avoid shadowing local names
body: Do not reuse an existing name for a different object in a nested scope unless there is a compelling reason. Use descriptive fresh names for intermediate facts and variables.

title: Use standard mathematical notation and statement forms
body: Prefer canonical forms such as `x ∈ s`, `s ⊆ t`, `{x | P x}`, `∀ x ∈ s, ...`, `∃ x ∈ s, ...`, `S.Nonempty`, `∑ x ∈ s, ...`, and `∃! x, ...`. State lemmas in the standard orientation and shape that Mathlib users expect.

title: Mark file-local implementation helpers `private`
body: Use `private` for helper lemmas and definitions that are not intended as part of the public API. Do not simulate privacy with leading underscores.

title: Order binders in standard Mathlib order
body: List type parameters first, then typeclass assumptions, then term arguments, then hypotheses. Keep dependent arguments after the data they depend on, and preserve established argument order unless there is a real API reason to change it.

title: Prefer `Type*` unless explicit universe control is needed
body: Write `Type*` rather than `Type _` or explicit universe parameters unless the declaration genuinely needs precise universe management.

title: Format `by` blocks and short proofs idiomatically
body: Use one-line proofs like `by rfl` or `by simp` when they are genuinely tiny. For nontrivial proofs, use a structured multiline `by` block rather than dense semicolon chains.

title: Break long declarations at logical boundaries
body: Keep short declarations on one line when they remain readable. For longer ones, break after binders or before the conclusion in a way that keeps the statement easy to scan.

title: Use `_root_` or `nonrec` only to avoid real shadowing problems
body:
Do not write `_root_.foo` unnecessarily, but do use `_root_` or `nonrec` when needed to refer to an existing declaration rather than a shadowing local one.
```lean
nonrec def min (a b : ℕ) : ℕ := _root_.min a b
```

title: Use `alias` for pure renamings and standard deprecation attributes
body: If a declaration is only another name for an existing theorem, use `alias` rather than restating or reproving it. When deprecating names, use the standard `@[deprecated (since := "...")]` form.

title: Use one blank line between top-level declarations
body: Separate consecutive top-level declarations with a single blank line. Avoid both cramped formatting and multiple blank lines.

title: Keep definitions computable when possible
body: Do not mark declarations `noncomputable` unless they genuinely require noncomputable machinery. Prefer constructive definitions when available.

title: Add type ascriptions for ambiguous numerals
body:
If Lean may infer the wrong type for a numeral, add an explicit type ascription or cast.
```lean
(1 : ℝ)
```

title: For metaprogramming, keep syntax lightweight and implementation structured
body: Use hygienic syntax and structured APIs rather than string hacking or disabled hygiene. Keep macros lightweight, separate syntax frontends from implementation logic, and reuse shared helpers instead of duplicating elaboration code.

title: Remove dead code and duplicates
body: Delete commented-out code, obsolete declarations, duplicate lemmas, unreachable branches, and unnecessary alternate names unless there is a clear API reason to keep them.

title: Avoid `rename_i`; name binders directly
body: Treat `rename_i` as a code smell. Name variables explicitly in binders, `intro` patterns, `match`, `induction`, or `next =>` clauses instead.

title: Keep changed code lint-clean
body: New or modified code should pass the relevant linters. Do not leave avoidable warnings behind.

title: Use standard binder notation
body: Prefer ordinary binder notation such as `∀ x, P x` and `Π x, A x` over awkward arrow encodings like `(x : α) -> ...` when binders are clearer.

title: Write explicit binder types
body: Prefer binders like `{R : Type*}` over bare `{R}`. Explicit binder types make signatures easier to read and review.

title: Prefer term-style definitions and pattern matching for data
body: When defining data or simple functions, use direct term syntax and `match` rather than tactic-mode `by` blocks or low-level recursors when possible. Reserve tactic mode for proofs or genuinely awkward definitions.

title: Do not commit placeholders or non-source artifacts
body: Do not commit `sorry`, generated build artifacts, or environment-specific files unless they are intentionally part of the source tree.

title: Prefer `<|` and `|>` over `$`
body: Use `<|` for right-associative application and `|>` for pipelines. Avoid `$` in Mathlib code.

title: Omit names for typeclass binders unless needed
body: Write `[Ring R]`, not `[hR : Ring R]`, unless the instance name is used in the proof. This keeps signatures shorter and clearer.

title: Keep imports minimal but sufficient
body: Add all required imports, remove redundant ones when practical, and avoid broad imports just to obtain a few names or notations.

title: Keep code readable and maintainable
body: Prefer clear, direct code over clever parser tricks, heavy golfing, or fragile definitional-equality arguments. If two versions work, choose the one a future contributor can read and modify easily.

title: Prefer standard Unicode notation and identifiers
body: Use established Mathlib Unicode forms such as `↦`, `≤`, `∅`, `𝓝`, `𝕜`, and subscripts like `h₂` instead of ad hoc ASCII alternatives. Avoid decorative Unicode in identifiers when it is not standard mathematical notation.

title: Prefer `≤` and `<` over `≥` and `>`
body: State inequalities with the smaller term on the left when practical. This matches common Mathlib normal forms and rewrites better.

title: Prefer named projections over numeric projections
body: Write `.val`, `.prop`, `.fst`, `.snd`, or a structure field name instead of `.1`, `.2`, `.3` when possible. Named projections are clearer and more robust.

title: Give top-level `def`s explicit return types
body:
Do not rely on inference for the result type of a top-level definition. Write the return type in the header.
```lean
def foo (x : α) : β := ...
```

title: Prefer `:= rfl` for definitional facts
body: If a declaration is definitionally true, write `:= rfl` rather than `:= by rfl`. This is shorter and keeps the definitional nature obvious.

title: Avoid `simp only` unless there is a reason
body: Do not replace ordinary terminal `simp` with `simp only` by default. Restrict the simp set only when maintainability, proof stability, or performance genuinely requires it.

title: Prefer direct terms over redundant wrapper syntax
body: If a proof or definition can be written cleanly as a direct term, do so instead of wrapping it in unnecessary `by exact ...`. Conversely, use `exact` inside tactic mode when it improves clarity.

title: Add useful standard attributes, and place them directly above the declaration
body: Use attributes such as `@[simp]`, `@[simps]`, `@[ext]`, and `@[to_additive]` when they provide standard automation or API support. Put attributes immediately above the declaration they modify.

title: Prefer direct syntax over verbose encodings
body: Use direct lambdas instead of `Function.const` when clearer, and use ordinary notation rather than low-level constants like `Nat.sub` or `Zero.zero` unless you specifically need them. Prefer `if`/`bif` over legacy `cond`.

title: Scope custom notation narrowly
body: Introduce custom notation only when it materially improves readability and is likely to be reused. Prefer `local notation`, `scoped notation`, or scoped infix declarations with explicit precedence, and avoid conflicts with existing syntax.

title: Use `where`, `termination_by`, and `decreasing_by` in standard layout
body: Indent `where` blocks consistently as part of the surrounding declaration, and keep `termination_by` and `decreasing_by` in the usual declaration layout. Do not use `where` when a simple direct term or record literal is clearer.

title: Keep lines within project limits
body: Break long lines rather than exceeding the repository’s line-length conventions, except for rare unavoidable cases such as URLs.

title: Use current Lean 4 / Mathlib syntax
body:
Prefer idiomatic Lean 4 / Mathlib forms and avoid legacy Lean 3 or mathport-style syntax such as `begin ... end` or obsolete patterns when a standard Lean 4 form exists.
```lean
theorem foo : P := by
  ...
```

title: Reuse existing structure data when constructing structures
body: When building a structure from an existing one, use structure extension or record update syntax such as `..` or `{ r with field := ... }` instead of restating unchanged fields manually.

title: Keep simp lemmas canonical
body: State `@[simp]` lemmas in the standard notation and coercion shape users will actually rewrite with. Avoid odd statement forms that make `simp` less effective or harder to discover.

title: Prefer `Array` over `List` for indexed or performance-sensitive code
body: Use `Array` when you need efficient indexed access or predictable performance. Reserve `List` for naturally recursive, proof-oriented, or list-like code.

title: Prefer direct application over unnecessary rewriting
body: If an existing lemma already matches the goal, apply it directly with `exact`, `refine`, `.mp`, `.mpr`, or other standard projections instead of rewriting through equivalent formulations for no reason.

title: Use explicit field names and standard record update syntax
body: Construct structures with named fields and update records with `{ r with field := ... }` rather than positional constructors or rebuilding unchanged fields manually.

title: Do not contort public statements just to get an `rfl` proof
body: Use `rfl` when it is the natural proof, but do not expose implementation details or choose unnatural statement forms merely so the proof can be reflexive.

title: Prefer standard mathematical and Mathlib terminology
body: Choose names from standard mathematical usage and existing Mathlib vocabulary. Do not invent ad hoc synonyms, and avoid names that would be confused with major existing concepts, tactics, or operations.

title: Keep one concept one name, and keep naming families parallel
body: Use one canonical public name per concept, and mirror naming across analogous APIs and variants (`Set`, `Finset`, `List`, `LinearMap`, `RingHom`, left/right, additive/multiplicative, dual, `Within`/global, etc.). If one member of a family is renamed, rename the corresponding variants consistently.

title: Make the name accurately match the statement
body: A declaration name must reflect the actual direction, hypotheses, operations, and conclusion of the theorem. If the statement changes, rename the declaration accordingly.

title: Preserve upstream and core names unless there is a compelling reason not to
body: Do not rename Lean core or upstream identifiers just to match Mathlib style. If a better Mathlib-facing name is needed, add an alias and document the reason.

title: Use standard theorem-name patterns
body: Follow established templates such as `_iff`, `_of_`, `_inj`, `_injective`, `_surjective`, `_bijective`, `_mono`, `_antitone`, `_eq_`, `_left`, and `_right`. Do not invent new patterns when a standard one already exists.

title: Mention only essential hypotheses in theorem names
body: Include assumptions in the name only when they distinguish the lemma from nearby variants, such as `_of_isUnit`, `_of_surjective`, or `_of_finite`. Omit routine, ambient, or implied hypotheses.

title: Follow Mathlib casing conventions
body: Use `lowerCamelCase` for term-level definitions, projections, and bundled maps; `UpperCamelCase` for types, structures, classes, namespaces, and files; and `snake_case` for theorem and lemma names.
