Preserve established acronym casing exactly as Mathlib does (`FG`, `ULift`, `ENNReal`, `ZMod`, etc.).

title: Reuse existing canonical names
body: Before adding a declaration, check whether Mathlib already has the same concept or statement under an established name. Reuse that name instead of creating synonyms, near-duplicates, or parallel terminology.

title: Include the main operation or construction in the name
body: If a statement is fundamentally about `map`, `comp`, `preimage`, `smul`, `pow`, `derivWithin`, or a similar operation, that operation should usually appear in the name. Avoid names that hide the central object of the lemma.

title: Put declarations in the most relevant namespace
body: Place lemmas and definitions in the namespace where users will look for them and where dot notation reads naturally. Avoid root-level names unless the declaration is truly global.

title: Distinguish variants with descriptive suffixes, not primes
body: When there are several similar lemmas, use explicit qualifiers such as `_univ`, `_singleton`, `_zero`, `_within`, `_id`, `_fun`, or `_pi`. Use `'` only for a very close entrenched variant when a descriptive name would be worse.

title: Prefer concise, guessable, statement-driven names
body: Use the shortest conventional name that remains clear. A user should be able to plausibly guess the name from the statement, and the name should reflect mathematical content rather than implementation details.

title: Support dot notation with namespace and argument order
body:
If a theorem or definition is naturally used as a method on a structure or hypothesis, put it in the corresponding namespace and make the receiver-like argument the first explicit argument.
```lean
namespace IsOpen
theorem preperfect (hU : IsOpen U) : ... := ...
end IsOpen
```

title: Use `_iff` only for actual `↔` lemmas
body: If the statement is an equivalence, use an `_iff` name; if it is not, do not. When useful, follow the `lhs_iff_rhs` pattern.

title: Use predicate-style names for Prop-valued declarations
body: Name `Prop`-valued definitions and classes as properties or predicates. Use `Is...` for Prop-valued mixins and properties when that is the local pattern, but not for data-bearing structures.

title: Use theorem-name segmentation that preserves existing API chunks
body: Theorem names should be `snake_case`, but if they contain an existing CamelCase declaration name, lowercase only its initial capital and keep the chunk intact. For example, prefer `liftRel_subrelation_lex`, not `lift_rel_subrelation_lex`.

title: Build theorem names from the statement, left to right
body: Arrange words in theorem names to follow the left-to-right order of the main symbols and arguments in the statement. For example, prefer `image_union` over `union_image`.

title: Name explicit instances with the `inst` prefix
body:
If an instance needs an explicit name, start it with `inst...`, and do not use `inst` for non-instances. If the instance will never be referenced by name, prefer leaving it unnamed.
```lean
instance instSubsingletonFoo : Subsingleton Foo := ...
```

title: Do not use underscores in definition names
body: Name definitions in `lowerCamelCase`, not `snake_case`. For example, write `shortComplexObj`, not `shortComplex_obj`.

title: Keep generated names sensible
body: When using `@[simps]`, `@[ext]`, `@[mk_iff]`, `@[to_additive]`, instance generation, or similar automation, choose source names so the generated declarations are conventional and informative. Override autogenerated names when they are unclear, ugly, or nonconforming.

title: Use conventional binder names and established argument order in statements
body: Within declarations, use standard variable names (`f g`, `x y`, `s t`, `R`, `M`, `ι`, etc.) and follow the established argument order of the surrounding API. Keep binder naming and ordering parallel across related lemmas.

title: Make conversion names show direction and structure
body:
For conversions and induced maps, make the direction obvious with names like `toX`, `ofX`, or `fromX`, and include the relevant structure when omission would be ambiguous.
```lean
def toLinearMap : ... := ...
def ofHom : ... := ...
```

title: Use standard constructor and helper names
body: Use `mk` for canonical constructors, `mk'` for established nearby constructor variants, and `of`/`ofX` for standard wrappers. Use `Core`, `Aux`, or `_aux` only for genuine internals, and keep even private helper names descriptive.

title: Do not encode local binder names into declaration names
body: Do not build theorem names from variable letters like `n`, `p`, `α`, or `q` unless they are part of an established concept. Name declarations after the mathematical objects and operations, not the chosen binder names.

title: Distinguish bundled and unbundled variants in names
body:
When the same underlying notion appears at different bundling levels, encode that in the name.
```lean
foo
fooHom
fooLinearMap
fooContinuousLinearMap
```

title: Rename public API only for a clear benefit
body: Do not rename declarations gratuitously. Rename only when the old name is misleading, conflicts badly, or clearly violates convention; when compatibility matters, add a deprecation or alias.

title: Use established names for standard operation families
body: Reuse Mathlib’s standard names for common operations and indexed/set variants: `image`, `preimage`, `union`, `inter`, `subset`, `compl`, `sup`, `inf`, `sSup`, `sInf`, `iSup`, `iInf`, `iUnion`, `iInter`, `pow`, `nsmul`, `smul`, `div`, `mod`, `nhds`, `nhdsWithin`, and so on. Do not invent abbreviations like `preimg`.

title: Use `_apply` for evaluation lemmas
body: If a lemma states the value of a function-like object at an argument, use the `_apply` suffix; use `_apply_apply` for doubly applied function-like objects. Do not use `_apply` for mere coercion lemmas or definitional unfolding.

title: Use `coe_...` for coercion lemmas, and name casts with the local cast vocabulary
body:
Name coercion lemmas with `coe_...` only when the coercion itself is the point; use `val` instead when the API is really about `Subtype.val`. For algebraic casts, follow the established local naming such as `natCast`, `intCast`, `ratCast`, or the prevailing `Nat.cast_*` family.
```lean
theorem coe_toLinearMap : ⇑φ.toLinearMap = φ := rfl
```

title: Name monotonicity lemmas consistently
body: Use `Monotone`, `Antitone`, `StrictMono`, and `StrictAnti` for properties, and theorem-name patterns like `_mono`, `_monotone`, or `.mono` according to the surrounding API. Keep related declarations parallel.

title: Distinguish `_inj` from `_injective`, and similarly for surjectivity
body: Use `foo_inj` for lemmas of the form `foo a = foo b ↔ a = b`, and `foo_injective` for lemmas concluding `Function.Injective foo`. Apply the same distinction to `_surj`/`_surjective` and related names when both forms exist.

title: Use `exists_...` for existential statements
body:
If the main content of the theorem is an existential claim, especially one beginning with `∃`, prefer an `exists_...` name.
```lean
theorem exists_mul_ne_zero : ∃ x, ... := ...
```

title: Do not repeat the namespace inside the identifier
body: Inside `namespace Foo`, write `bar`, not `fooBar` or `FooBar`, unless extra disambiguation is genuinely necessary.

title: Name rewrite lemmas after the expression being rewritten
body:
For equality/rewrite lemmas, choose the name based on the expression users will search for or rewrite, usually the left-hand side. If appropriate, orient the equality so the more complicated expression is on the left.
```lean
theorem coeff_sum : coeff (∑ i in s, f i) n = ...
```

title: Use `_left` and `_right` systematically for side-specific variants
body: For binary operations or side-specific statements, use `_left` and `_right` to indicate which side varies, is fixed, or is cancelled. Provide both variants when symmetry does not make one redundant.

title: Use `..._of_...` for implication lemmas
body:
Reserve `of` for names read as “conclusion from hypothesis,” and put the conclusion before the hypothesis: `conclusion_of_hypothesis`.
```lean
theorem finite_of_injective : Function.Injective f → Finite α := ...
```

title: Use only established abbreviations
body: Use established Mathlib abbreviations such as `fg`, `finrank`, `zpow`, `nsmul`, `rpow`, `logDeriv`, and `supp`. Do not invent ad hoc shortenings.

title: Distinguish equivalences from categorical isomorphisms
body: Use `equiv` for equivalences (`α ≃ β`) and `iso` for categorical isomorphisms (`X ≅ Y`). Name conversions between related notions in the obvious direction.

title: Use words like `zero`, `one`, `top`, and `bot` in names
body: Prefer identifier words over numerals or symbols in declaration names, such as `map_zero`, `pow_two`, and `eq_top_iff`. Avoid arbitrary numerals unless there is an established family.

title: Name structure fields and projections predictably
body:
Structure fields are term-level names, so use `lowerCamelCase`. Field names should match the conventional theorem or property names they encode so the generated API is predictable.
```lean
structure Equiv where
  toFun : α → β
  left_inv : ...
```

title: Use `to...` only for genuine projections or conversions
body: Reserve `toX` names for canonical projections, forgetful maps, or explicit conversions. Do not use `to...` for arbitrary constructions unrelated to forgetting structure.

title: Use `comp` consistently for composition lemmas
body: If a statement is about composition, include `comp` in the name and place it to reflect the composition order in the statement. Do not mention `comp` when the theorem is not really about composition.

title: Follow local conventions for specialized families
body: For specialized APIs, follow entrenched local naming rather than inventing a new pattern. Examples include `symm` placement, `succ` vs `add_one`, `prodMk`, `fun_`/`_pi`, and other family-specific conventions.

title: Make names `to_additive`-friendly
body: Choose multiplicative names so the additive translation is sensible, and provide an explicit additive name when the automatic result would be poor. Likewise, keep dualized names (`dual`, `_op`, `_unop`) aligned with established patterns.

title: Use singular names for predicates and plurals for collections
body: A predicate on elements should usually be singular or adjectival, while plural names are for bundled collections or sets. For example, `Prime : Nat → Prop` but `primes : Set Nat`.

title: Use established category names and abbreviations
body:
For bundled categories, use the standard short name if one already exists (`Grp`, `Ring`, `Top`, ...); otherwise use the structure name with `Cat`.
```lean
LatticeCat
```

title: Use standard projection and sum-side names
body: Use `fst`/`snd` for product projections and `inl`/`inr` for coproduct sides. Do not replace these with `left`/`right` where Mathlib already has a standard term.

title: Keep tactic, elaborator, syntax, and command names descriptive and non-conflicting
body: Name tactics and elaborators by behavior and scope, mirroring analogous existing tools. Give custom syntax explicit names with `syntax (name := ...)`, and avoid `#`-prefixed names for persistent commands.

title: Name `Tendsto` lemmas with `tendsto_...` when that is the main content
body: For lemmas whose main content is a `Tendsto` statement, prefer a `tendsto_...` prefix unless an established namespace method is better. Include filters such as `atTop`, `atBot`, `nhds`, or `nhdsWithin` when they distinguish the result.

title: Reserve `ext` for genuine extensionality lemmas
body: Use `ext` only for lemmas characterizing equality of arbitrary objects by their fields or evaluations. Put such lemmas in the relevant namespace, not `_root_`.

title: Reserve `refl`, `symm`, `trans`, `assoc`, and `comm` for their standard roles
body: Use these suffixes only when the theorem really expresses reflexivity, symmetry, transitivity, reassociation, or commutativity. In particular, use `_assoc` for reassociated variants and `_comm` for swapping symmetric arguments or commutative laws.

title: Use `₀` only for established zero-variant families
body: Reserve subscript `₀` for standard Mathlib families such as `MonoidWithZero` variants. Do not introduce ad hoc `₀` names.

title: Use `_def` for definitional unfolding lemmas
body: When a theorem simply unfolds a definition to its defining expression, `_def` is an appropriate suffix.

title: Use `congr` only for congruence lemmas
body: Reserve `congr`, `congrLeft`, and `congrRight` for lemmas about congruence or transport along equalities/equivalences. Do not use `congr` for arbitrary maps or unrelated equivalences.

title: Do not distinguish public names only by case or a trailing underscore
body: Avoid identifiers whose only difference is capitalization, and do not use trailing `_` in public names. Choose a genuinely different, readable name instead.

title: Avoid `_root_` unless it is genuinely needed
body: Do not use `_root_` to work around poor namespace choices. Use it only when the intended public API really belongs in the root or another parent namespace.

title: Use `_eq_` or `_eq` only when equality is genuinely the point
body: Use `_eq_` when the key content is an equality between two named notions, and use a trailing `_eq` only when it actually disambiguates. Do not append `_eq` mechanically to every equality theorem.

title: Use conventional names for induction principles and recursors
body: Follow established names such as `induction_on` rather than `inductionOn` for theorem-style induction principles. Name recursor arguments and cases conventionally (`major`, `minor`, `zero`, `succ`, `cons`, etc.).

title: Distinguish type-level and subset-level properties consistently
body: When both a property of a type and a property of subsets exist, follow the established pairing and do not blur them. For example, use `CompactSpace` for the type-level property and `IsCompact` for subsets.

title: Prefer American English and usually ASCII in identifiers
body: Use American spellings in declaration names, and prefer ASCII unless a non-ASCII form is already established. For example, prefer `center`, `neighborhood`, and `color`.

title: Use `Stronger.toWeaker` for explicit inheritance maps
body:
When naming explicit inheritance or forgetful instances outside the `inst...` pattern, use `Stronger.toWeaker`.
```lean
instance CommRing.toRing : Ring R := ...
```

title: Use `map_...` for homomorphism-preservation lemmas
body: For lemmas stating that a homomorphism preserves an operation or constant, use the standard `map_*` pattern, such as `map_add`, `map_mul`, or `map_zero`.

title: Use `protected` when a short standard name should live in a namespace
body: If a declaration should have a conventional short name but needs namespacing to avoid clashes, use `protected` where appropriate.

title: Use established suffixes for default-value variants
body:
When a family already uses `D` for an explicit default argument and `I` for an `Inhabited`-provided default, follow that convention consistently.
```lean
headD
headI
```

title: Keep theorem docstrings about the result, not the proof
body: Use theorem and lemma docstrings to describe the statement and intended use. Put proof sketches, tactic commentary, and implementation details in ordinary comments nearby or in module-level `

title: State assumptions, caveats, and exact behavior accurately
body: Mention non-obvious hypotheses, quantifiers, coercions, and limitations, and ensure the docstring matches the code exactly. Do not claim more generality than the declaration has.

title: Document new public declarations when the docstring adds real value
body: Add `/-- ... -/` docstrings to new public, user-facing declarations: definitions, structures, classes, important instances, notation, syntax, tactics, commands, and nontrivial theorems/lemmas. Do not use placeholder docstrings or text that merely restates the name; trivial lemmas whose name and statement are already completely clear may omit a docstring.

title: Keep module docstrings high-level and synchronized with the file
body: Module docs should describe the file’s actual contents and main ideas, not PR history or future promises. Update them when the file gains or changes a central theorem, definition, notation, or other major export.

title: Write self-contained docstrings
body: Make each docstring understandable in hover view without relying on surrounding file context. Do not say “above”, “below”, or “this” ambiguously; name the relevant declaration, object, or parameter explicitly.

title: Use standard mathematical terminology and Mathlib naming
body: Prefer established mathematical terminology and existing Mathlib conventions over ad hoc wording or obscure abbreviations. If a declaration name is descriptive rather than standard, mention the standard term or common alias when that improves discoverability.

title: Add a module docstring to every new substantive file
body: Every nontrivial new file should have a top-level `/-! ... -/` module docstring after the imports. It should begin with a `#` title and briefly explain the file’s mathematical purpose, main definitions, and major results.

title: Describe mathematical meaning, behavior, and purpose
body: Explain what the declaration means mathematically, how it behaves, and why a user would use it. Do not merely paraphrase the type signature or identifier.

title: Follow normal sentence-style punctuation and capitalization
body: Capitalize the first word of a docstring. End complete sentences with periods, and use consistent punctuation style within a file.

title: Use the correct doc comment form and placement
body: Use `/-- ... -/` for declaration docstrings and `/-! ... -/` for module docstrings. Place module docstrings immediately after imports, and place declaration docstrings directly on the declaration they document.

title: Explain how variants differ
body: When introducing `foo'`, left/right variants, specialized/generalized versions, converses, or similar nearby declarations, say explicitly how they differ in hypotheses, domain, codomain, or formulation.

title: Put documentation on the canonical user-facing declaration
body:
Attach the main docstring to the declaration users should hover or search for, not to a wrapper, helper, or re-export. For aliases and notation with the same meaning, prefer `@[inherit_doc]` rather than duplicating text.
```lean
@[inherit_doc foo]
notation:50 "⌣" => foo
```

title: Cross-reference closely related declarations
body: Use `See also` or similar wording to point to converses, special cases, generalizations, additive/multiplicative counterparts, and alternative APIs. This is especially useful when several nearby declarations are easy to confuse.

title: Keep terminology consistent within the file and API
body: Use one term consistently for the same concept rather than alternating between synonyms. Match existing Mathlib terminology unless there is a strong reason not to.

title: Add comments to long, delicate, or non-obvious proofs
body: If a proof is long, structurally subtle, or performance-sensitive, add brief comments explaining the main steps or why the proof is written that way. Avoid comments that merely restate the current goal or obvious tactic effects.

title: Format docstrings so generated docs render correctly
body: Use renderer-friendly Markdown/CommonMark, with sensible indentation for wrapped paragraphs and lists. Avoid malformed formatting, broken spacing, or ad hoc HTML that harms generated documentation.

title: Prefer fully qualified names in docstring references
body:
When referring to other declarations, prefer fully qualified backticked names when that improves reliability of doc-gen links or avoids ambiguity.
```lean
`CategoryTheory.Functor.map`
```

title: Mark internal or auxiliary declarations as such
body:
If a declaration is private, auxiliary, or an implementation detail not meant for direct use, say so and point users to the preferred public API when relevant.
```lean
/-- Implementation detail. Users should use `foo` instead. -/
def fooAux ...
```

title: Use comments for local rationale, not as a substitute for docs
body: Use ordinary comments for local explanations, workarounds, proof strategy, and maintenance notes. Keep them close to the code they explain, and do not use them in place of declaration or module docstrings.

title: Explain non-obvious parameters, fields, and return values
body: Document parameters, indices, bundled data, structure fields, and return values when their role is not clear from the type alone. For `Bool`/`Option`-valued declarations, say exactly what `true`/`false` or `some`/`none` means.

title: Use backticks for Lean identifiers and valid Lean code
body: Wrap declaration names, notation, and Lean expressions in backticks in docstrings and comments. Only backtick valid Lean identifiers or expressions; otherwise use plain prose or a fenced code block.

title: Add characteristic lemmas for new definitions and operations
body: When introducing a new `def`, also provide the key lemmas that characterize or compute it, such as `_iff`, `_def`, `_apply`, or `@[simp]` lemmas. If a simp lemma mainly supports automation or a chosen normal form, document that intent when useful.

title: Document tactics, commands, attributes, and tools at the user-facing entry point
body: Ensure the thing users invoke has the docstring, not only internal helpers. Describe behavior, syntax, options, limitations, and how it differs from nearby commands or tactics.

title: Use standard deprecation mechanisms with explicit replacements
body:
When deprecating a declaration, use `@[deprecated newName]` or the appropriate deprecation form so users see the replacement. Include `since := "YYYY-MM-DD"` when required, and say explicitly if there is no direct replacement.
```lean
@[deprecated newFoo (since := "2025-01-15")]
def oldFoo := newFoo
```

title: Structure module docstrings with standard sections
body: Use standard `##` headings such as `

title: Record TODOs and temporary workarounds clearly
body: Put important future work in a module docstring or a clearly marked nearby `TODO` comment, with enough context to revisit it later. Remove or update TODOs once they are no longer accurate.

title: Document instances and coercions by their mathematical meaning
body: For instances and coercions, explain the intended mathematical relationship between the source and target structures, not just the implementation. If typeclass behavior is surprising, document that too.

title: Keep docstrings concise and readable
body: Use clear natural English, put the key point first, and keep one-line docstrings on one line when possible. Add detail only when it genuinely helps users understand the API.

title: Document linter, checker, and tool behavior precisely
body: For linters and similar tools, state what pattern they detect, why it is discouraged, what they guarantee, and any important limitations or false positives/false negatives.

title: Use Lean/Mathlib notation and Unicode in documentation
body: Prefer Lean-friendly notation and standard Unicode already used in Mathlib, such as `f ⁻¹' s`, `Icc a b`, `ℝ`, `≤`, and `∘`. Avoid LaTeX-heavy prose in declaration docstrings unless Lean notation would be unreasonable.

title: Add examples to tactic, command, syntax, and notation docs
body: For tactics, commands, macros, syntax extensions, and unusual notation, include at least one concrete example of typical usage. Explain supported inputs, important options, goal shapes or outputs, and notable failure modes when relevant.

title: Document notation in module docs and relevant declaration docs
body: If a file introduces important notation or scoped notation, document it in a `

title: Prefer fixing documentation over suppressing doc lints
body: Do not use `@[nolint]` or `@[inherit_doc]` as a generic escape hatch for missing or poor documentation. Add real documentation instead; reserve suppressions for genuine deliberate exceptions.

title: Preserve useful existing documentation and avoid unnecessary churn
body: Do not remove, weaken, or rewrite good existing documentation just for stylistic preference. In a focused PR, prefer targeted fixes to inaccurate or unclear docs over broad unrelated rewrites.

title: References` section or a declaration docstring using Mathlib’s standard citation style. Reuse existing bibliography entries and standard references where possible.
Document omitted instances and non-obvious typeclass behavior
body: If a natural instance is intentionally omitted to avoid loops, diamonds, or poor inference, explain why. Also comment on surprising `inferInstance`, local instance, or inference-chain behavior when users or maintainers would otherwise be confused.

title: Document edge cases and default or junk values explicitly
body:
If a definition returns a default value outside its intended domain, or has important degenerate cases, say so explicitly in the docstring.
```lean
/-- Returns `0` when the support is infinite. -/
def finsumLike ...
```

title: Use standardized `Porting note:` comments for intentional port divergences
body: When a Lean 4 port intentionally differs from the source in a non-mechanical way, add a nearby comment beginning exactly with `Porting note:` and explain what changed and why. Remove or update the note when it becomes obsolete.

title: Document notation and syntax by meaning, not just parsing
body: User-facing notation and syntax should explain what they mean mathematically, not only how they parse. For unusual binders or sigils, include an explicit expansion or example when helpful.

title: Implementation notes` or nearby comments. Help users and maintainers understand why the API is designed that way.
Explain every lint suppression or intentional exception
body: Whenever you use `@[nolint]`, suppress a warning, or keep an intentional exception, add a nearby comment explaining why. The explanation should help a future maintainer decide whether to remove the suppression or keep it.

title: Use `library_note` only for reusable library-wide rationale
body: Create a `library_note` only for design guidance that matters across multiple files or the whole library. Reference it with the standard mechanism such as `#see_note`.

title: Add references for major mathematical content
body: For important definitions, theorems, or files, include literature references in a `

title: Give stable names to instances or generated declarations when docs must refer to them
body: If documentation or links need to mention an instance or generated declaration, give it an explicit stable name rather than relying on autogenerated names. This improves hover text, linking, and maintainability.

title: Implementation notes` or comments for non-obvious design choices
body: Explain surprising representations, omitted instances, one-sided APIs, inference workarounds, arbitrary choices, or performance-driven decisions in `

title: Use precise, informative error messages in user-facing metaprograms
body: When throwing errors in `MetaM`, tactics, commands, or other user-facing metaprogramming code, provide specific messages explaining what failed and what the user should check. Avoid generic placeholders like `"failed"`.

title: Use fenced code blocks for examples, syntax, and diagrams
body: When including examples, syntax forms, or ASCII diagrams in docstrings, wrap them in triple backticks so they render correctly in generated documentation.

title: Use `@[stacks "TAG"]` for Stacks Project references
body: When a result corresponds to the Stacks Project, attach the exact `@[stacks "TAG"]` attribute with the correct tag.

title: Keep author and copyright metadata minimal, standard, and accurate
body: Use the project’s standard header format for new files. Only add authors for substantial file-level contributions, and do not casually change copyright headers or years for routine edits.

title: Explain unusual proof/code tricks that remain necessary
body: If final code contains non-obvious constructs such as `_`, `change`, terminal `simp only`, unusual parser forms, or delicate elaboration tricks, add a short comment explaining why they are needed.

title: Reserve `example` for tests and documentation
body: Use `example` for tests or demonstrations, especially inside docstrings or test files. Do not use unexplained `example` declarations as reusable library API when a named theorem, lemma, or private helper would be clearer.

title: Use Mathlib interval notation, not informal bracket notation
body: Write intervals as `Ioo a b`, `Ioc a b`, `Ici a`, and so on, not `(a, b)` or `[a, ∞)`. This avoids ambiguity and matches Mathlib style.

title: Notation` section and mention any required `open scoped`. Also document the notation on the relevant declaration when that helps users understand what it expands to.
Use `def` for data and `theorem`/`lemma` for proofs
body: Use `def` when constructing mathematical data such as an equivalence, morphism, or object. Use `theorem` or `lemma` for mathematical results, and do not use `def` merely to package proof code.

title: Implementation notes`.
Tags` when applicable. List the key exported declarations rather than every auxiliary lemma.
Communicate module deprecations explicitly
body: If a module is removed or renamed, leave a deprecation mechanism in the old location telling users what to import instead. Do not make downstream users guess the replacement module.

title: Implementation notes`, `
Main definitions`, `
Notation`, `
Use `
Use the most specific existing namespace
body:
Put each declaration in the established namespace matching the primary structure it is about, and prefer an existing namespace over inventing a new one. Choose the most specific namespace that still reflects the intended API.
```lean
namespace ContinuousLinearMap
theorem my_lemma (f : E →L[𝕜] F) : ... := ...
end ContinuousLinearMap
```

title: Reuse existing definitions and keep one canonical abstraction
body: Before adding a new definition, check whether Mathlib already has the concept. Prefer expressing specialized constructions as instances, abbreviations, aliases, or corollaries of existing general machinery rather than duplicating parallel API.

title: Place declarations in the most specific appropriate module
body: Put each definition, lemma, or instance in the file whose mathematical topic it belongs to, not merely where it happens to compile. Use tools like `#find_home!` when unsure.

title: Keep new declarations out of `_root_`
body:
Place new definitions, lemmas, and instances in the namespace of the main mathematical object or theory, not in `_root_`, unless they are truly global API. This improves discoverability and avoids name collisions.
```lean
namespace Submodule
theorem foo ... := ...
end Submodule
```

title: Keep PRs small, focused, and reviewable
body: Each PR should have one coherent purpose. Separate mechanical refactors, file moves, import cleanup, and renames from substantive API or mathematical changes, and split large projects into incremental merge-ready stages.

title: Group shared assumptions with `section` and `variable`
body:
When multiple consecutive declarations share parameters or typeclass assumptions, introduce them once in a `section` with `variable` declarations instead of repeating binders on every theorem.
```lean
section CommRing
variable {R : Type*} [CommRing R]
...
end CommRing
```

title: Keep internal helpers out of the public API
body:
Mark proof-specific or implementation-only helpers as `private` when they are only internal details. If a result is mathematically reusable outside the file, make it public and place it in the appropriate module instead.
```lean
private theorem aux ... := ...
```

title: Minimize imports and keep dependency direction clean
body: Import the smallest set of modules needed for the file, and avoid adding heavy or transitive-only imports to foundational files. If one lemma requires heavier imports than the rest of the file, move that lemma to a downstream file instead of bloating a basic module.

title: Keep module structure coherent and well-scoped
body: A file should read as a coherent Lean module, not an ad hoc script with repeated top-level namespace hopping. Ensure `namespace` and `section` blocks are properly opened and closed with matching `end`s.

title: Place declarations near related material
body: Put new lemmas and definitions next to the existing declarations they extend or characterize. Basic API lemmas should appear immediately after the definition they describe, and related lemmas should stay in one contiguous block.

title: Keep module hierarchy clear, canonical, and non-redundant
body: Organize modules by the main mathematical object or logical strength, using established Mathlib hierarchy and naming. Avoid fake module paths, unclear duplication, or excessive directory nesting unless the split is stable and meaningful.

title: Follow local naming, statement, and attribute conventions
body: Match the naming scheme, argument order, namespace placement, `@[simp]` usage, and overall style of nearby analogous declarations. New API should look like it belongs in the file and theory where it is added.

title: Open namespaces and notation only at the narrowest useful scope
body:
Prefer `open X in`, local `open`, `open scoped`, or `open Classical in` over file-wide `open`, unless the whole file genuinely relies on it. This limits namespace and instance pollution.
```lean
open scoped Classical in
theorem foo : _ := ...
```

title: Land prerequisites and broad refactors separately
body: If a change depends on a missing general lemma, new abstraction, or large reorganization, introduce that in a preliminary or follow-up PR rather than burying it inside a domain-specific change. Avoid broad downstream churn unless that refactor is itself the purpose of the PR.

title: Preserve compatibility when renaming declarations or modules
body:
When renaming or moving declarations, provide `@[deprecated]` aliases; when moving files, leave a deprecated shim module at the old path when appropriate. Remove deprecated names only after the normal grace period.
```lean
@[deprecated new_name (since := "2026-01-01")]
alias old_name := new_name
```

title: Enforce recurring structural rules with linters and CI
body: If a style or hygiene issue recurs, prefer a linter or CI check over reviewer memory. Integrate checks into standard Mathlib or Lake entrypoints, and reduce false positives rather than disabling checks wholesale.

title: Keep variable and section scopes minimal
body: Declare only the variables and instances actually needed, and close sections as soon as their assumptions are no longer required. Avoid carrying unused assumptions through later declarations or redeclaring variables already in scope.

title: Avoid unnecessary or overly deep namespaces
body: Do not introduce wrapper namespaces that add no semantic value or force users through internal paths. Prefer flatter, discoverable namespace structure unless deeper nesting reflects a real conceptual distinction.

title: State results in canonical form under the weakest natural assumptions
body: Use the library’s standard formulation and the weakest typeclass assumptions that actually suffice. Put a lemma in the weakest section or file where it is valid, but do not over-generalize if the broader abstraction would require disproportionate new infrastructure.

title: Split large or mixed-purpose files along conceptual boundaries
body: If a file becomes large or mixes foundational material with heavier theory, split it into smaller modules along conceptual and dependency boundaries. Keep lighter imports available without forcing downstream users to import advanced results.

title: Factor repeated proof patterns into reusable lemmas
body: If the same argument or intermediate result appears repeatedly, extract it into a reusable lemma in the appropriate shared module instead of reproving it ad hoc. Keep it local only if it is not useful elsewhere.

title: Prefer one central general lemma over many special cases
body: When several statements are all consequences of a more natural general theorem, prove the central result and derive the special cases from it. Avoid combinatorial API growth from many near-duplicate variants.

title: Use `protected` for method-like lemmas when it avoids collisions
body:
Mark lemmas `protected` when they are naturally part of a structure’s API and should be accessed as `X.lemma`, especially to avoid clashes with generic names. Do not use `protected` to hide broadly useful standalone lemmas without a clear reason.
```lean
namespace Foo
@[protected] theorem bar : True := trivial
end Foo
```

title: Scope notation, syntax, and specialized instances
body: Prefer `scoped` notation, scoped instances, and scoped parser or macro declarations over global ones. Do not introduce broad global notation or non-canonical global instances in shared modules.

title: Place tactic and metaprogramming code in the proper hierarchy
body: Put user-facing tactics under `Mathlib/Tactic` and keep metaprogramming utilities separate from mathematical theory. Do not mix tactic or linter infrastructure into mathematical namespaces.

title: Use `public section` and `@[expose]` only when genuinely needed
body: Use `public section` when a whole block is intentionally exported, and use `@[expose]` only when downstream code truly needs definitional unfolding of the implementation body. Do not add extra exposure machinery when ordinary visibility suffices.

title: Keep `Defs` files lightweight
body: In `Defs` files, include only core definitions, essential instances, and very basic `rfl`-style lemmas. Move theorem-heavy API and advanced results to `Basic` or later files to preserve a light import graph.

title: Keep root and widely imported modules conservative
body: Do not add broad imports to `Mathlib.Init` or other root files without strong justification. Changes there affect the whole library and should remain minimal and stable.

title: Avoid redundant API and thin wrapper lemmas
body: Do not add lemmas or definitions that merely restate obvious consequences of existing API unless they are genuinely useful for automation or repeated use. When a stronger lemma subsumes an older one, deprecate, alias, or remove the redundant version instead of keeping both.

title: Separate results by assumption strength
body: When different groups of lemmas require different typeclass assumptions, organize them into separate sections or files reflecting those levels. This keeps weaker results easy to find and prevents stronger assumptions from leaking into unrelated API.

title: Extract reusable lemmas from long instance or construction proofs
body: If an instance or construction requires a long proof, factor out supporting lemmas instead of keeping everything inline. This improves readability and often creates useful API for later files.

title: Use `namespace` blocks instead of manual name prefixes
body: When several declarations share a prefix, open a `namespace X ... end X` block rather than naming everything `X.foo`, `X.bar`, etc. Inside `namespace X`, write `foo`, not `X.foo`, unless qualification is needed for disambiguation.

title: Avoid unnecessary or fragmented sections
body: Do not create a `section` for a single declaration or merely to save a little typing. Merge adjacent sections with the same context, and use theorem-local binders when assumptions are needed only once.

title: Place instances near the definitions they belong to
body: Declare instances immediately after the definition or general instance they specialize, and keep related instances grouped together. Do not scatter instance declarations throughout the file.

title: Split modules by dependency weight
body: Separate lightweight definitions and core instances from proof-heavy, tactic-heavy, or specialized developments. Use stable splits such as `X.Defs`, `X.Basic`, and later files when that keeps imports lighter and the hierarchy clearer.

title: Keep the public API surface minimal and stable
body: Expose clean characterization lemmas rather than implementation-heavy details, and avoid forcing users to rely on unfolding internal definitions. A new definition should come with the basic lemmas or instances needed to make it usable, but not a large cloud of redundant wrappers.

title: Use `public import` or `export` only for intentional re-exports
body:
Prefer ordinary `import` unless downstream users should automatically receive the imported module through the current file. Use `export` or `public import` only when that re-export is part of the module’s intended API.
```lean
namespace MyNS
export OtherNS (usefulLemma)
end MyNS
```

title: Keep files focused and place declarations in the right module
body: Each file should have a coherent mathematical theme. Put declarations in the file whose topic and dependency level best match them, rather than wherever the needed imports already happen to be available.

title: Scope instances carefully and keep them canonical
body: Global instances should be canonical and broadly appropriate. Use `local instance` or `scoped instance` for specialized or potentially conflicting instances, and place reusable public instances in the canonical namespace and file near related definitions.

title: Name sections after the assumptions they introduce
body: Give sections meaningful names reflecting the extra assumptions in scope, such as `section CommRing`, rather than vague topic labels. Put the corresponding `variable` declarations immediately after the section header so the scope is obvious.

title: Avoid unnecessary churn in widely used names, modules, and style
body: Do not rename or relocate foundational concepts, core modules, or heavily used identifiers without strong justification. Likewise, avoid widespread style-only edits unless the cleanup is systematic, local, and directly justified by the PR.

title: Order declarations from foundational to derived
body: Introduce basic definitions and supporting lemmas before higher-level constructions and main theorems that depend on them. Keep the file’s narrative aligned with dependency order.

title: Avoid import-time computation and side effects
body: Importing a module should not perform expensive computation or fragile initialization. Keep generated or cached artifacts out of the critical import path, and ensure importing Mathlib yields a usable environment.

title: Use `namespace` for naming scope and `section` for variable scope
body: Use `namespace` when you want qualified names and dot-notation; use `section` when you want local parameters or assumptions. Do not use one as a substitute for the other.

title: Keep bundled objects bundled when that is the standard API
body: State results using the standard bundled representation or canonical type used elsewhere in Mathlib, rather than an equivalent but less standard encoding. Do not unbundle maps into bare functions when downstream API expects bundled morphisms.

title: Keep tests lightweight and close to what they validate
body: Use dedicated test files or Lake components with minimal imports and stable runtime. Put regression tests near the relevant definition when practical, and avoid tests that rely on large rebuild surfaces or intentionally slow compilation.

title: Inline one-off trivial helpers
body: If a helper lemma is tiny and used only once, prove it inline rather than adding a standalone declaration. Extract a separate lemma only when it improves reuse, readability, or API.

title: Keep binder and universe order consistent
body: When related declarations repeat binders, preserve the established order of variables, universes, and typeclass assumptions. Avoid arbitrary reordering that creates unnecessary churn or downstream breakage.

title: Generate symmetric APIs automatically when possible
body:
For paired additive/multiplicative or order-dual results, state one canonical lemma and derive the others with automation such as `@[to_additive]` or `@[to_dual]` when available. If the surrounding API already provides both variants manually, keep new additions consistent with that pattern.
```lean
@[to_dual] theorem max_def ... := ...
```

title: Provide standard extensionality lemmas when needed
body: For new structures, add the minimal useful `ext` or `hom_ext` lemmas needed for standard extensionality tooling. Do not add a large family of extensionality lemmas unless they are genuinely used.

title: Use environment extensions for persistent tooling state
body: For registries, caches, “already processed” markers, or other persistent metaprogramming state, use environment extensions rather than ad hoc global state. Follow existing `register...Extension` patterns.

title: Use `noncomputable section` for noncomputable developments
body: When a file or substantial block is noncomputable, wrap it in a `noncomputable section` rather than tagging each declaration individually. Do this only when the noncomputability genuinely applies to the whole block.

title: Use section headers and comments to reflect real structure
body: In long files, organize material with meaningful comment headers and section names that match the mathematical grouping. Avoid stale or arbitrary headers that do not reflect the actual contents.

title: Prefer definitional equality and coherent instance inheritance
body: When designing derived instances, make them definitionally equal to existing constructions whenever possible. Avoid diamonds, shortcut instances, or inheritance patterns that create multiple non-defeq versions of the same structure.

title: Use standard simplification and aliasing mechanisms
body: Mark genuinely useful rewrite lemmas with `@[simp]` instead of duplicating simplification logic elsewhere. When introducing a new name for an existing theorem, use `alias` rather than restating the theorem.

title: Restrict `meta` to the smallest necessary scope
body: Do not mark an entire file or large section as `meta` if only a few declarations need it. Scope `meta` as narrowly as possible, especially outside tactic-specific files.

title: Ensure new modules are compiled and indexed
body: After adding new files, make sure they are reachable from the project’s import structure so CI and users actually compile them. In Mathlib, run `lake exe mk_all` and include the resulting updates to `Mathlib.lean` in the same change.

title: Use `assert_not_exists` to protect lightweight files
body: In foundational files where heavy dependencies must not appear, add `assert_not_exists` checks using the most specific forbidden name that captures the architectural constraint. This helps prevent accidental import bloat.

title: Use `abbrev` for lightweight aliases
body: If a definition is just a convenient synonym and does not need its own reducibility or API story, prefer `abbrev` over a new `def`. This keeps the development lightweight and avoids unnecessary API commitments.

title: State results under the weakest assumptions actually used
body: Use the minimal typeclasses and hypotheses required by the proof. Prefer `Semiring` over `Ring`, `Preorder` over `LinearOrder`, `Finite` over `Fintype`, `Injective` over `Bijective`, and weaker side conditions such as `≤` or `x ≠ 0` when stronger ones are unnecessary.

title: Generalize to the most natural useful form, but not speculatively
body: Generalize over types, parameters, indices, domains, codomains, filters, and measures when the proof supports a broader mathematically natural statement. Do not introduce extra abstraction merely for hypothetical future use if it makes the API harder to understand.

title: Prove the general theorem first; derive special cases afterward
body: When a specialized lemma is needed, first prove the underlying general result and obtain the special case by instantiation, rewriting, or a short corollary. Do not duplicate the proof for the special case.

title: Make non-inferable key parameters explicit; inferable ones implicit
body: If Lean can infer a parameter reliably, prefer an implicit binder. If a parameter is not determined by the others, is central to rewriting, or must be chosen by the user, make it explicit.

title: Extract reusable intermediate facts, but inline one-off helpers
body: If a local `have` captures a nontrivial fact likely to be reused, factor it into a named lemma with appropriate generality. Conversely, do not create standalone lemmas for tiny one-use proof steps that only add noise.

title: Reuse existing API; do not add redundant declarations
body: Before adding a new lemma, definition, or instance, check whether the result already already exists, is a direct specialization of an existing theorem, or is definitionally equal to something already in Mathlib. Do not add thin wrappers like `lemma foo := by simpa using bar`; use `bar` directly unless a specialized corollary clearly improves usability.

title: Add the expected symmetric, dual, and parallel variants
body: When a lemma belongs to a standard family, provide the corresponding left/right, dual, additive/multiplicative, image/preimage, `WithinAt`/`At`, `norm`/`nnnorm`, or similar variants when natural. Do not leave the API asymmetrical without a reason.

title: Use one canonical representation; avoid duplicate APIs
body: Choose a canonical spelling or normal form for each concept and state the main lemmas there. Provide translation lemmas for coercion variants or equivalent formulations instead of maintaining parallel theorem families for the same idea.

title: Prefer `↔` characterizations for genuine equivalences
body: When both directions are natural, state the result as an `iff` and use an `_iff` name. Prefer `P ↔ Q` over `P = Q` for propositions, since `iff` lemmas work better with `rw`, `simp`, `.mp`, and `.mpr`.

title: Use `Type*` by default and keep universe polymorphism clean
body: Prefer `Type*` or omitted universe annotations in ordinary developments. Introduce explicit universe parameters only when needed, avoid unnecessary universe clutter or forced equalities like `u = v`, and ensure declarations do not leave unresolved universe metavariables.

title: Use automation and transport mechanisms for parallel APIs
body: Prefer `@[to_additive]`, `OrderDual`, standard coercion lemmas, and other transport infrastructure over manually duplicating proofs. Reuse existing machinery for additive, dual, opposite, restricted, semilinear, or scalar-tower variants when possible.

title: Prefer local or filter-based hypotheses when the conclusion is local
body: For within-set, neighborhood, asymptotic, or convergence statements, state assumptions on the same set or filter as the conclusion. Prefer forms like `EqOn`, `ContDiffOn`, `∀ᶠ x in l, ...`, and `Tendsto` over unnecessarily global hypotheses.

title: Remove unused, redundant, and proof-only hypotheses
body: Every binder should affect the statement, another binder’s type, or typeclass inference. Drop assumptions implied by others, and if decidability or classical choice is only needed in the proof, use local `classical` rather than adding it to the theorem statement.

title: Handle degenerate and boundary cases explicitly instead of assuming them away
body: Avoid unnecessary assumptions like `n ≠ 0`, `Nonempty α`, or positivity hypotheses when the zero, empty, or boundary case can be handled directly by `by_cases`, `cases`, or `simp`. Include such assumptions only when the statement genuinely fails without them.

title: Do not rely on fragile definitional equality
body: Avoid proofs that depend on implementation-specific `rfl` or defeq behavior across abstraction boundaries, bundled/unbundled forms, opposites, quotients, or coercions. Use explicit API lemmas and transport lemmas so proofs remain stable under refactors.

title: Keep definitions minimal; move assumptions to lemmas
body: Do not bake unnecessary finiteness, decidability, nonemptiness, or side conditions into definitions. Put such requirements on the theorems that need them instead.

title: Scope `variable` declarations and assumptions tightly
body: Do not overuse broad section-wide `variable` blocks. Introduce shared parameters and stronger typeclass assumptions only where they first become necessary, so later declarations are not accidentally over-generalized or over-constrained.

title: Prefer the right abstraction level, not a stronger or more concrete one
body: State results for the simplest abstraction that captures the proof. Prefer `LinearMap` over `LinearEquiv` if invertibility is unused, `Embedding` or `Injective` over `Equiv` if no inverse is needed, and `Set`-based formulations over encoded ranges or subtype-heavy statements when the subtype itself is not essential.

title: Keep specialized convenience corollaries when they improve usability
body: After proving a general theorem, keep or add short specialized lemmas for common cases if they improve discoverability or avoid awkward instantiations. These corollaries should be derived from the general result, not reproved.

title: Use canonical Mathlib predicates, structures, and abstractions
body: State results using standard notions such as `Set.EqOn`, `Disjoint`, `Monotone`, `StrictMono`, `BijOn`, `IsLUB`, `Pairwise`, `Finite`, `Finsupp`, and `Nodup` rather than ad hoc encodings. Reuse existing Mathlib abstractions before introducing new wrappers, predicates, or typeclasses.

title: Preserve established binder order, explicitness, naming, and statement shape
body: When generalizing or refactoring an existing theorem, keep variable names, binder order, explicitness, and overall shape aligned with nearby API unless there is a strong reason to change them. Consistency improves search, rewriting, and downstream compatibility.

title: Split `iff` directions when they need different hypotheses
body: If one direction of an equivalence needs weaker assumptions than the other, prove separate directional lemmas at their natural strength and combine them into the final `iff` only under the stronger assumptions.

title: For `iff` lemmas, keep arguments mostly implicit
body: In `↔` lemmas, variables appearing on both sides should usually be implicit so users can write `rw [foo_iff]` or use `h.mp` and `h.mpr` without supplying extra arguments.

title: Prove results at the right categorical level, then specialize
body: If a result is genuinely categorical, prove it in the general categorical setting and derive concrete corollaries afterward. When computational content is needed, work with explicit cones, cocones, or chosen limit data rather than mere existential existence assumptions.

title: Prefer canonical left-hand sides and rewrite-friendly statement forms
body: Orient equalities and `iff` lemmas so the left-hand side is the expression users naturally encounter in goals. Choose forms that work well with `rw` and `simp`, and keep key rewrite parameters explicit when needed.

title: State the strongest natural conclusion the proof gives
body: If the proof naturally yields an equality rather than an inequality, an `iff` rather than an implication, or a stronger structural conclusion such as `Subsingleton`, state that stronger result and derive weaker corollaries afterward.

title: Use proposition-level finiteness and the right cardinality notion
body: Prefer `[Finite α]`, `s.Finite`, `Nat.card`, or `Set.ncard` when only finiteness or cardinality is needed, and obtain a `Fintype` locally only if the proof requires computation. Use `Finset.card` for explicit finite data and `Nat.card` for types.

title: Split conjunctions and independent conclusions into separate lemmas
body: Avoid bundling reusable facts into a single theorem with conclusion `A ∧ B` unless the conjunction itself is the natural API. Separate existence from uniqueness, and give focused lemmas for each reusable component.

title: Add foundational API for new definitions and structures
body: A new definition should usually come with the expected characterization lemmas, `ext` or `ext_iff`, coercion and `*_apply` lemmas, simp lemmas for special values, and compatibility with standard operations. Do not leave a bare definition without the basic API users will need.

title: Prefer `abbrev`, notation, or canonical definitions over parallel wrappers
body: If a new `def` would only create an unnecessary abstraction barrier, prefer `abbrev`, notation, or a single canonical definition with specialized lemmas. Collapse near-duplicate definitions instead of maintaining parallel wrappers.

title: Prove independence lemmas for choice-based definitions
body: If a definition depends on an auxiliary choice such as a basis, representative, or witness, prove that the resulting object or property is independent of that choice. Use those independence lemmas downstream instead of repeatedly unfolding the choice.

title: Keep proofs maintainable and robust
body: Prefer direct, modular proofs over brittle cleverness or dependence on accidental elaboration behavior. Add explicit type annotations when needed, use `wlog ... generalizing ...` and induction with proper generalization, and avoid gratuitous `generalize`.

title: Preserve useful definitional equalities when they are cheap and intentional
body: If a construction is meant to reduce by `rfl` in common cases and preserving that behavior is easy, prefer the design that keeps the useful definitional equality. But do not contort the API solely to force defeq when a clean abstraction plus explicit lemmas is better.

title: Preserve downstream compatibility when changing existing API
body: When generalizing or refactoring an existing theorem, avoid unnecessary breaking changes in argument order, explicitness, names, or statement shape. Keep old specialized lemmas as wrappers or deprecations when appropriate, and separate renames from signature changes.

title: Keep PR scope focused
body: Do not mix unrelated refactors, infrastructure changes, renames, and new theorems in a single PR. Large generalizations, file moves, or compatibility changes should usually be split into separate PRs.

title: Prefer set-level formulations over finitary encodings when the mathematics is set-based
body: Do not force a `Finset` or `List` representation when the statement is really about sets or unordered collections. Use `Set`, `Set.Finite`, `Multiset`, or `Finset` according to whether order and multiplicity matter.

title: Keep naming, notation, and namespace behavior conservative and predictable
body: Follow existing Mathlib naming conventions and avoid introducing competing naming styles. Add notation only when it is standard, unambiguous, and genuinely improves readability, and keep name resolution unsurprising under ordinary namespace usage.

title: State hypotheses in the weakest natural user-facing form
body: Choose assumptions users are likely to already have and that match the real dependency of the proof. For example, prefer `Preconnected` over `Connected`, `SurjOn` over global surjectivity, `x ≠ 0` over `0 < x` when order is irrelevant, and `IsUnit x` when invertibility is the real assumption.

title: Prefer arbitrary finite index types over hard-coded `Fin n`
body: If a result is conceptually about a finite family, state it for a general index type `ι` with `[Finite ι]` or `[Fintype ι]` rather than only `Fin n`, unless fixed-size indexing itself is essential.

title: Keep theorem statements semantically precise and free of proof artifacts
body: Do not expose arbitrary choices, chosen witnesses, auxiliary points, implementation details, or intermediate constructions that arose only in the proof. Public statements should mention only the real mathematical data and dependencies.

title: In category theory, prefer isomorphism over equality of objects
body: Use `Iso` or equivalence-style statements rather than literal equality of categorical objects unless equality is truly the right notion. This avoids unnecessary transport and matches standard categorical practice.

title: Generalize filter and measure statements appropriately
body: State convergence lemmas for arbitrary filters when the proof only uses filter properties. In measure theory, parameterize results by an explicit measure `(μ : Measure α)` on a `MeasurableSpace α` rather than hard-coding `volume` or requiring a bundled `MeasureSpace` unless that is genuinely intended.

title: Keep theorem statements usable after simplification
body: When a theorem is intended to be used after `simp` or `dsimp`, shape its arguments and conclusion so it matches the form that actually appears in goals. Add eta-expanded or composed variants only when rewriting genuinely needs them.

title: Shape simp lemmas to be canonical and composable
body: State `@[simp]` lemmas so they rewrite toward the canonical normal form users want and compose well with arbitrary simp sets. Do not add redundant specialized simp lemmas when a more general one already covers the case.

title: Prefer ambient-type membership hypotheses over subtype formulations
body: When a theorem is about elements of a set or substructure, prefer hypotheses like `x ∈ s` over introducing subtype variables `(x : s)` unless the subtype itself is essential. This reduces coercion friction and usually yields a more reusable statement.

title: Avoid syntactic tautology lemmas
body: Do not add lemmas whose two sides are definitionally the same or that merely restate syntax after unfolding or coercion simplification. Remove such lemmas instead of suppressing lints.

title: Design APIs so extensionality and automation are clean
body: Choose representations that make the main `ext` theorem natural and easy to use. If many proofs are repetitive projection-by-projection arguments, add appropriate `@[ext]`, `@[simps]`, or related automation rather than duplicating boilerplate.

title: Avoid awkward `Nat` subtraction in statements
body: Remember that `Nat` subtraction is truncated. Prefer addition-based reparameterizations such as `n = k + t`, `n + 1`, or equivalent formulations that avoid brittle `n - k` expressions in theorem statements.

title: Order binders idiomatically
body: Put type parameters and main objects first, then auxiliary data and hypotheses. Match the order users naturally encounter in expressions and the order used by related lemmas.

title: Use direct coercion and normalization lemmas when needed
body:
If downstream use needs a specific coercion equality, state it explicitly in canonical form instead of relying on unfolding. For example, a lemma like
```lean
@[simp] lemma coe_toFoo : ((f.toFoo : _) : α → β) = f := rfl
```
is preferable to expecting users to unfold definitions manually.

title: Use `TFAE` for many equivalent characterizations
body: When proving several equivalent formulations of the same property, prefer a `TFAE` block over a large family of pairwise `iff` lemmas. Add only the most useful named consequences afterward.

title: Prefer direct, curried hypotheses over bundled existential or conjunctive assumptions
body: If users naturally supply separate facts or witnesses, take them as separate arguments rather than hiding them inside `A ∧ B`, `∃ x, ...`, or implication-shaped hypotheses. Prefer `(ha : A) (hb : B)` over `(h : A ∧ B)` and `∀ x ∈ s, P x` over subtype-heavy packaging.

title: Use `rcases` and `rintro` when destructuring with generalization
body: When pattern matching while also generalizing dependent variables, prefer `rcases` and `rintro` over plain `cases` and `intro` when they produce a cleaner and more robust proof state.

title: Match theorem names to their actual mathematical content
body: Ensure the statement has the intended strength and the name accurately describes what it proves. Do not give a theorem a name suggesting a stronger or different result than its actual conclusion.

title: Keep imports, parsing, and dependency footprint tidy
body: Avoid unnecessary imports and dependency growth, especially in foundational files. When porting or refactoring, preserve the intended parsed meaning with explicit parentheses if needed rather than relying on changed parser behavior.

title: In metaprograms, distinguish local variables from metavariables
body: Do not identify variables by user-facing names alone or conflate free variables with metavariables. Use stable identifiers such as `FVarId`, and only create fresh metavariables when the local context has actually changed.

title: For proof-oriented fixed-size data, prefer `Fin n → α` over `Vector α n`
body: In theorem statements and proofs, `Fin n → α` is often easier to use than `Vector α n`. Reserve `Vector` for APIs where the computational representation matters, and provide conversions if both views are useful.

title: Put declarations in the most general appropriate namespace and file
body: If a result is not specific to the current specialized context, place it in the more general namespace or earlier foundational file where it belongs. Avoid duplicating specialized versions in later files when a reusable general statement is possible.

title: Preserve metadata and deprecation behavior in generated or migrated APIs
body: Automatically generated or migrated declarations should preserve relevant attributes, naming conventions, and deprecation tags. When deprecating APIs, provide a reasonable transition path rather than removing them immediately.

title: Use the weakest sufficient typeclass assumptions
body: State lemmas, definitions, and instances with the minimal algebraic, order, topological, or categorical assumptions actually used. Prefer `[Semiring R]` over `[Ring R]`, `[Preorder α]` over `[LinearOrder α]`, and `[AddCommMonoid M]` over `[AddCommGroup M]` unless stronger structure is genuinely needed.

title: Use `letI`/`haveI` only for actual local instances
body: Introduce local instances with `letI` or `haveI` only when typeclass inference should use them. For ordinary auxiliary facts or terms, use `let` or `have` instead.

title: Use explicit type ascriptions or `inferInstanceAs` to guide inference
body: When elaboration or typeclass search is ambiguous, add a type annotation, named arguments, or `inferInstanceAs` with the intended target type. Prefer this to strengthening theorem assumptions or relying on fragile elaborator guesses.

title: Declare something as an `instance` only if typeclass search should use it
body: Use `instance` only for results intended to be found automatically by inference. If a result is not meant to drive search, or is better used explicitly, declare it as a `lemma` or `def` instead.

title: Prefer `Type*`/`Sort*` by default; follow local universe conventions in sensitive areas
body: Use implicit universe polymorphism with `Type*` or `Sort*` in ordinary declarations. Add explicit universe binders only when necessary, and in areas like category theory match the surrounding Mathlib universe and instance conventions exactly.

title: Use typeclass arguments only for assumptions meant to be inferred
body: Write a hypothesis as `[P]` only if instance search is expected to supply it. If a parameter or proposition is not realistically inferable, keep it as an explicit argument.

title: Prefer standard bundled assumptions when they already exist
body: Use the canonical Mathlib class that packages the intended structure instead of ad hoc combinations of hypotheses. For example, prefer `[IsDomain R]` over `[Nontrivial R] [NoZeroDivisors R]` when that is the real assumption.

title: Introduce new typeclasses only when inference is genuinely useful
body: Do not turn every reusable property into a class. Use a `class` only when there is a canonical instance and automatic inference or propagation through constructions is part of the intended API; otherwise prefer `structure`, `def`, `abbrev`, or an explicit proposition.

title: Avoid unnecessary decidability assumptions
body: Do not add `[DecidableEq α]`, `[DecidablePred p]`, or `[Decidable p]` unless the statement or definition genuinely depends on computable decidability. If decidability is only needed internally, use `by classical` or a local instance.

title: Avoid duplicate, overlapping, or looping instances
body: Before adding an instance, check whether `inferInstance` already finds one or whether a more general existing instance subsumes it. Do not introduce instances that create diamonds, circular search, or broad fallback behavior; use a lemma or a wrapper instead if necessary.

title: Remove redundant assumptions implied by stronger ones
body: Do not list typeclass assumptions already implied by others in context. For example, omit `[Nontrivial R]` if `[IsDomain R]` is assumed, and do not restate additive structure already provided by `[Module R M]`.

title: Use the standard hierarchy’s intended classes
body: Prefer existing Mathlib hierarchy nodes such as `SMulCommClass`, `IsScalarTower`, `ContinuousSMul`, `CovariantClass`, and `ContravariantClass` over custom parallel classes. Also choose the weakest standard class that matches the proof.

title: Set explicit priorities for overlapping instances
body: When an instance may overlap with others, assign an explicit priority so more specific instances are tried before generic fallback ones. Broad bridge instances should usually have lower priority.

title: Preserve definitional equality in instance design
body: Design inheritance and derived instances so there is a single canonical path to each structure, and important inherited fields remain definitionally equal to the intended parent instances. Avoid refactors that break `rfl`-based behavior or change which instance path Lean uses.

title: Make inferable parameters implicit, but keep ambiguous structure parameters explicit
body: Make parameters implicit when Lean can reliably recover them from later arguments or instance search. Keep them explicit when inference is unreliable or when multiple instances may exist on the same type, especially for data-carrying structures like `MeasurableSpace` or topologies.

title: Prefer `Finite` to `Fintype` unless enumeration is required
body: Use `[Finite α]` when only finiteness is needed mathematically, and require `[Fintype α]` only when the statement or definition uses computable data such as `Fintype.card`, `Finset.univ`, or explicit enumeration. Derive a local `Fintype` with `letI := Fintype.ofFinite α` when needed in a proof.

title: Control reducibility deliberately in typeclass-facing definitions
body: If instance search should find an instance by unfolding a wrapper, use `abbrev` or `@[reducible]` intentionally. If unfolding would be harmful or noncanonical, keep the definition opaque.

title: Choose `abbrev`, `def`, and one-field `structure` intentionally
body: Use `abbrev` for transparent aliases that should inherit reducibility and existing instances. Use a one-field `structure` when you want a genuinely distinct type with separate instances; do not use `def` as a fake newtype.

title: Prefer direct shortcut instances over long inference chains
body: If typeclass search repeatedly reaches a target through a long chain of intermediate instances, add a direct canonical shortcut instance or refactor the hierarchy. This improves performance and predictability.

title: Use standard typeclass formulations for common properties
body: Prefer canonical Mathlib classes and predicates over ad hoc hypotheses. Examples: use `[NeZero n]` instead of `h : n ≠ 0`, `Set.Nonempty s` instead of `Nonempty s`, and standard notions like `Commute`, `Monotone`, or `RightInverse`.

title: Keep `Classical` and `noncomputable` as local as possible
body: Use `classical` or `open Classical` only in the smallest scope that needs it, and mark only the smallest appropriate definition or instance as `noncomputable`. Do not add `noncomputable` to `Prop`-valued declarations.

title: Scope noncanonical or context-dependent instances narrowly
body: If an instance is noncanonical, expensive, or only useful in one context, make it `local`, `scoped`, or attach it to a wrapper type rather than declaring it globally.

title: Ensure instance parameters are genuinely inferable
body: Do not introduce instance arguments whose applicability depends on unresolved metavariables or ambiguous inputs. Use `outParam` or `semiOutParam` only when a parameter is truly determined by the others.

title: Use standard coercion classes and keep coercions canonical
body: Define coercions through the appropriate coercion classes such as `Coe`, `CoeSort`, and `CoeFun`, and ensure the coercion really has the advertised type. Avoid ad hoc or misleading coercions.

title: Use constructor-shaped instance definitions when defeq matters
body: Prefer `instance ... where` or direct structure literals, explicitly assigning parent fields when necessary to preserve definitional equality. Record updates are often better than helper functions for keeping constructor shape clear.

title: Keep proof-only assumptions local
body: If `DecidableEq`, `DecidablePred`, `Classical`, `Nontrivial`, `Fact p`, or similar assumptions are needed only inside the proof, introduce them locally with `classical`, `letI`, or `haveI` rather than strengthening the theorem statement.

title: Prefer `FunLike`/`DFunLike` and `SetLike` interfaces
body: For function-like structures, provide the standard `FunLike` or `DFunLike` interface; for set-like subobjects, provide `SetLike`. Rely on these standard interfaces instead of custom coercions, membership relations, or parallel order structures.

title: Extend only minimal compatible superclasses
body: When defining a class with `extends`, include only the weakest superclasses whose operations and axioms genuinely match your structure. If the natural stronger superclass is too strong, extend a weaker one and add stronger instances separately.

title: Reuse canonical instances with `inferInstance` and projections
body: When an instance already exists, use `inferInstance`, `inferInstanceAs`, or parent projections instead of manually rebuilding it from fields. Forget parent structure via projections; do not reconstruct it.

title: Use `class` for canonical inferable structure and `structure` for explicit data
body: If data is meant to be passed explicitly rather than synthesized by typeclass search, define it as a `structure` rather than a `class`. Avoid data-carrying typeclasses for noncanonical choices.

title: Make property classes `Prop`-valued mixins
body: If a class expresses a reusable law or property rather than data, define it as `: Prop`, typically with an `Is...` name or as a small mixin. Keep optional laws such as commutativity, cancellation, or compatibility separate from core data-carrying classes.

title: Order typeclass arguments to reflect dependencies and help search
body: List foundational classes before structures that depend on them, and compatibility classes after the structures they relate. In instance declarations, put more discriminating assumptions earlier so search can fail fast.

title: Usually leave instances unnamed, but name them if they must be referenced
body: In general, let Lean use instances structurally rather than by generated names. Give an instance an explicit stable name only if you will later disable it, reprioritize it, or refer to it directly.

title: Use `Fact` sparingly and usually locally
body: Avoid global `Fact` instances unless they are extremely safe and canonical. Prefer explicit hypotheses, or introduce `haveI : Fact p := ⟨hp⟩` locally when `Fact` is only needed to trigger existing API.

title: Keep theorem APIs and instance APIs separate
body: Do not make ordinary theorems into instances or use instances as disguised lemmas. If a fact is useful both as a theorem and for inference, provide both forms explicitly.

title: Register companion instances expected by the hierarchy
body: When defining a new structure or action that should interact with standard automation, also provide the expected companion instances or lemmas. For example, a new `SMul` instance often also needs `SMulCommClass` or `IsScalarTower` if those laws hold.

title: Use `to_additive` carefully and define multiplicative versions first
body: When using `@[to_additive]`, define the multiplicative declaration first and ensure helper declarations, parent classes, and coercion-related names translate correctly. Add explicit `to_additive` mappings when automation does not choose the right names.

title: Avoid overlapping coercions to functions
body: Do not define multiple competing coercions from the same structure to function types. Route function coercions through the canonical `FunLike.coe` path.

title: Prefer existing standard wrappers
body: Use standard wrappers such as `Subtype`, `Opposite`, `Lex`, `WithOne`, and similar constructions when they fit the intended meaning. Do not invent a custom wrapper when Mathlib already provides the right one with established API and instances.

title: Keep global instances narrow, canonical, and fast
body: Avoid broad, variable-headed, or expensive global instances that match too many goals. An instance should either apply quickly or fail quickly.

title: Avoid pattern matching on typeclass instances
body: Do not destructure instances directly in proofs or definitions. Use typeclass inference, explicit arguments, projections, or lemmas about the class instead.

title: Use wrappers when one carrier needs multiple incompatible structures
body: If the same underlying type needs different global instances, introduce a wrapper or new bundled type and put the alternative instances there. Do not install competing global instances on the same type.

title: Prefer `Nonempty` over `Inhabited` for mere existence
body: Use `Nonempty α` when only existence matters. Assume or define `Inhabited α` only when a distinguished default element is genuinely part of the API.

title: Prefer concrete bundled morphism types in APIs
body: In theorem statements and definitions, use concrete morphism types such as `MonoidHom`, `RingHom`, `LinearMap`, or `ContinuousLinearMap` rather than `...HomClass` unless the extra abstraction is genuinely needed.

title: Handle measurable-space assumptions carefully
body: In measure-theoretic APIs, do not force a global `[MeasureSpace α]` when an explicit measure `μ` and a measurable-space structure suffice. Keep measurable-space parameters controlled and explicit enough when multiple sigma-algebras may exist on the same type.

title: Keep numeral support canonical and total
body: Use `OfNat` only when numerals have a total, canonical interpretation on the type. Do not define numeral instances for types that interpret only some numerals or whose numeral semantics are nonstandard.

title: Define foundational operations before higher-level structure
body: Introduce basic operations such as `Zero`, `Add`, `Mul`, or `SMul` before defining structures like `Ring`, `Module`, or similar classes that depend on them. Ensure higher-level structures use the intended underlying operations definitionally.

title: Avoid `let` bindings inside `instance` definitions
body: Inside an `instance`, avoid local `let` bindings that complicate unfolding and search. If helper terms are needed, move them outside or use `letI` only for genuine local instances.

title: Use `extends` only when parameters align cleanly
body: Use bundled inheritance when the subclass naturally extends a superclass on the same parameters. If parameters do not line up well, prefer explicit fields or unbundled mixins over awkward projection instances.

title: Use `deriving` for straightforward standard instances
